events.text: The patient is a 71-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is of Eastern European descent, retired steelworker, non-smoker, with a significant family history of cancer including a brother with pancreatic cancer and a sister with breast cancer (BRCA2-associated pedigree). He has comorbidities of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus (well-controlled), and hyperlipidemia.

At age 68 years, the patient had a diagnosis of stage IV (T3b N1 M1b, AJCC 8th edition) adenocarcinoma of the prostate (ICD-O-3 site code C61.9, histology code 8140/3, Gleason score 9 [4+5], Grade Group 5). Summary stage: distant. The tumor involved the seminal vesicles bilaterally and there was a single right obturator lymph node metastasis (1.2 cm) as well as multiple bone metastases involving the lumbar spine (L3, L4), right iliac wing, and right femoral neck. PSA at diagnosis: 187 ng/mL. Relevant biomarkers: androgen receptor positive, no neuroendocrine differentiation. Confirmation: positive histology via transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate needle biopsy.

At age 68 years, the patient had a prostate needle biopsy pathology result. Specimen: transrectal ultrasound-guided 12-core prostate needle biopsy. Findings: adenocarcinoma of the prostate involving 10 of 12 cores bilaterally, Gleason score 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5. Perineural invasion identified. No lymphovascular invasion. IHC: PSA positive, NKX3.1 positive, androgen receptor strongly positive, synaptophysin negative, chromogranin negative, p53 wild-type pattern.

At age 68 years, the patient had a technetium-99m bone scan and CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis for staging. Bone scan showed multiple foci of increased radiotracer uptake consistent with osseous metastatic disease at L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck. CT abdomen/pelvis showed an enlarged right obturator lymph node measuring 1.2 cm (short axis), and no visceral metastases. CT chest showed no pulmonary metastases. Prostate gland measured 45 cc with extracapsular extension and seminal vesicle invasion.

At age 68 years, the patient had germline genetic testing performed (blood-based) and somatic next-generation sequencing performed on the prostate biopsy specimen. Germline testing showed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22), consistent with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome. Somatic NGS panel (84-gene panel) showed: BRCA2 p.Ser1982Argfs*22 (likely reflecting loss of heterozygosity with somatic second hit, VAF 78%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 42%), PTEN loss (homozygous deletion), RB1 loss (heterozygous deletion), MYC gain (copy number 6), CDK4 gain, FOXA1 p.R265Q (VAF 31%), SPOP p.F133V (VAF 18%), ATM wild-type, CDK12 wild-type, CHEK2 wild-type, AR wild-type, TMPRSS2-ERG fusion not detected. TMB: 3 mutations/Mb (low). MSI: microsatellite stable. PD-L1: CPS 2.

At age 68 years, the patient initiated androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg intramuscular injection every 3 months plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally once daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily (mHSPC intensification per LATITUDE/STAMPEDE data). Castrate testosterone levels confirmed at 6 weeks (testosterone < 20 ng/dL). Initial PSA response: PSA declined from 187 ng/mL to 3.2 ng/mL at 3 months and nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 6 months.

At age 69 years (12 months after starting ADT/abiraterone), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA was 1.1 ng/mL, testosterone < 20 ng/dL confirming castrate state. Bone scan showed stable disease with no new lesions. The patient tolerated therapy well with mild fatigue (Grade 1) and mild hypertension requiring medication adjustment. Performance status ECOG 1. Disease status: cancer present, responding to therapy (biochemical and radiographic response).

At age 69 years and 6 months, the patient had a restaging bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis. Bone scan showed previously identified lesions at L3, L4, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck had improved (sclerotic changes consistent with treatment response) with no new osseous metastases. CT abdomen/pelvis showed the right obturator lymph node had decreased to 0.7 cm. No visceral metastases. Findings consistent with partial response to ADT/abiraterone.

At age 70 years (approximately 24 months after starting ADT/abiraterone), the patient had an oncologist office assessment for rising PSA. PSA had risen from nadir of 0.8 ng/mL to 4.7 ng/mL over 3 consecutive measurements 4 weeks apart, fulfilling PCWG3 criteria for PSA progression. Testosterone remained castrate (< 20 ng/dL). The patient reported increased back pain (Grade 2, L3-L4 region) requiring scheduled acetaminophen. Performance status ECOG 1. Abiraterone was continued pending restaging imaging. Diagnosis of castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) established.

At age 70 years, the patient had a restaging PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) for evaluation of castration-resistant progression. Findings: increased PSMA avidity at previously identified L3 and L4 vertebral lesions, new PSMA-avid lesions at T10 vertebral body, right posterior 7th rib, left posterior 9th rib, and right sacral ala. Enlarged right obturator lymph node now measures 1.4 cm with increased PSMA avidity. No visceral metastases. Findings consistent with osseous disease progression (new bone metastases) with nodal involvement. No PSMA-negative lesions of concern.

At age 70 years, the patient discontinued abiraterone/prednisone and was initiated on enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily. ADT (leuprolide) was continued. The rationale included CRPC with bone-dominant disease, prior abiraterone exposure limiting re-use, no visceral crisis, and BRCA2 mutation (noting olaparib as a future option upon enzalutamide progression given BRCA2 status and PROfound trial data). Baseline PSA: 4.7 ng/mL. Baseline denosumab 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks was added for bone protection (with calcium and vitamin D supplementation).

At age 70 years and 2 months after starting enzalutamide, the patient had a follow-up oncologist office assessment. PSA declined to 1.9 ng/mL (59% decline from baseline). The patient reported fatigue (Grade 2) and mild cognitive slowing (Grade 1). No falls or seizures. Blood pressure well-controlled. Performance status ECOG 1. Disease status: cancer present, responding biochemically to enzalutamide. Continued enzalutamide 160 mg daily.

At age 70 years and 3 months after starting enzalutamide, the patient experienced a Grade 2 fatigue adverse event (CTCAE v5.0), attributed to enzalutamide. Additionally, the patient developed a Grade 1 fall event (single episode, no injury). No dose reduction was required. Supportive measures initiated including physical therapy referral and fall prevention counseling. The patient also reported Grade 1 hot flashes related to ongoing ADT.

At age 70 years and 5 months after starting enzalutamide, the patient had a restaging assessment. PSA nadir on enzalutamide reached 0.6 ng/mL. Bone scan showed stable previously identified lesions, no new lesions. CT abdomen/pelvis showed stable right obturator lymph node (0.9 cm). Back pain had improved significantly. Performance status ECOG 1. Disease status: cancer present, best response of partial response (biochemical and radiographic). Continued enzalutamide.

At age 70 years and 5 months after starting enzalutamide, the patient had a bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis for response assessment. Bone scan showed previously identified osseous lesions at L3, L4, T10, ribs, and sacrum appeared stable with sclerotic changes. No new osseous lesions. CT abdomen/pelvis showed the right obturator lymph node measured 0.9 cm (down from 1.4 cm). No new lymph node enlargement. No visceral metastases. Consistent with stable disease to partial response on enzalutamide.

At age 71 years (approximately 6 months after starting enzalutamide), the patient had an oncologist office assessment documenting enzalutamide progression. PSA had risen from nadir of 0.6 ng/mL to 8.3 ng/mL over 3 consecutive monthly measurements. The patient reported worsening back pain (Grade 2) and new right hip pain. Performance status ECOG 1-2. Restaging imaging ordered. Disease status: cancer present, progressing on enzalutamide.

At age 71 years, the patient had a restaging PSMA PET/CT. Findings: marked progression with numerous new PSMA-avid osseous metastases including new lesions at the right femoral head/neck (previously treated lesion now larger), bilateral iliac crests, L1-L2 vertebral bodies, sternum, and multiple ribs (5 new rib lesions). Increased size of right obturator lymph node (now 2.1 cm). No visceral metastases identified. No PSMA-negative lesions. Findings consistent with substantial bone and nodal disease progression.

At age 71 years, the patient had a CT-guided bone biopsy of the right iliac crest lesion performed to evaluate for treatment resistance mechanisms and confirm adenocarcinoma histology prior to PARP inhibitor therapy. Result: metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason grade group 5. No neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (synaptophysin negative, chromogranin negative). AR positive (IHC). Ki-67 proliferation index 35%. BRCA2 loss confirmed by FISH (homozygous deletion).

At age 71 years, the patient discontinued enzalutamide following confirmed radiographic and biochemical progression. Given germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant with confirmed somatic loss of heterozygosity (biallelic BRCA2 loss), the patient was initiated on olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily (per FDA approval for BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC, PROfound trial). ADT (leuprolide) was continued. Denosumab was continued for bone protection. Baseline PSA at olaparib start: 8.3 ng/mL. Baseline CBC: Hgb 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 K/uL, WBC 6.1 K/uL.

At age 71 years and 6 weeks after starting olaparib, the patient had a follow-up assessment. PSA had declined to 4.1 ng/mL (51% decline). The patient reported significant fatigue (Grade 2) and nausea (Grade 1, improved with anti-emetics). CBC showed Hgb 10.2 g/dL (declined from 11.8 at baseline), platelets 176 K/uL. Right hip pain improving (Grade 1). Performance status ECOG 1. Disease status: cancer present, early biochemical response to olaparib. Continued olaparib at full dose.

At age 71 years and 3 months after starting olaparib, the patient developed clinically significant anemia (Grade 3, CTCAE v5.0). Hemoglobin declined to 7.8 g/dL. The patient was symptomatic with dyspnea on exertion and fatigue. Reticulocyte count low (1.1%), consistent with hypoproliferative anemia (olaparib-related myelosuppression). Iron studies: ferritin 68 ng/mL, TIBC 320 mcg/dL, serum iron 45 mcg/dL — borderline iron deficiency as a contributing factor. B12 and folate normal. Hemolysis workup negative. Olaparib dose reduced to 200 mg twice daily.

At age 71 years and 3 months after starting olaparib, the patient required packed red blood cell (pRBC) transfusion: 2 units pRBCs administered for symptomatic Grade 3 anemia (Hgb 7.8 g/dL). Post-transfusion Hgb rose to 10.1 g/dL. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose 750 mg) administered to address concurrent iron deficiency. Olaparib dose reduced to 200 mg twice daily. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not initiated due to malignancy context and thromboembolic risk.

At age 71 years and 4 months after starting olaparib, the patient had a follow-up assessment post-dose reduction and transfusion. PSA was 2.8 ng/mL (continued biochemical response despite dose reduction). Hgb 9.6 g/dL (stable post-transfusion). The patient reported improved fatigue (Grade 1) and nausea resolved. Performance status ECOG 1. Bone pain minimal (Grade 1). Disease status: cancer present, responding to olaparib (reduced dose). Continued olaparib 200 mg twice daily.

At age 71 years and 5 months after starting olaparib, the patient had a restaging bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Bone scan showed improvement in intensity of uptake at multiple previously identified osseous sites. CT showed the right obturator lymph node had decreased to 1.2 cm. CT chest showed no pulmonary nodules or parenchymal metastases. CT abdomen showed no hepatic lesions. Findings consistent with partial response to olaparib.

At age 71 years and 6 months after starting olaparib, the patient required a second packed red blood cell transfusion (2 units pRBCs) for recurrent symptomatic anemia (Hgb 7.6 g/dL, Grade 3). The anemia was again attributed to olaparib-related myelosuppression. Platelets remained adequate (142 K/uL). WBC 3.8 K/uL (Grade 1 leukopenia). Repeated IV iron supplementation administered. Hematology consultation obtained to co-manage myelosuppression.

At age 71 years and 8 months after starting olaparib, the patient had an oncologist office assessment documenting concern for disease progression. PSA had risen from nadir of 2.1 ng/mL to 14.6 ng/mL. The patient reported new right upper quadrant discomfort and a 6-lb unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks. Physical examination revealed mild hepatomegaly. Performance status ECOG 2. Restaging imaging ordered urgently. Disease status: cancer present, progression on olaparib suspected.

At age 71 years and 8 months after starting olaparib, the patient had a CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast for urgent restaging. Findings: new hepatic metastases identified — 3 lesions in the right hepatic lobe (largest 2.8 cm) and 1 lesion in the left lobe (1.4 cm). New pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe (0.9 cm) and left upper lobe (0.7 cm), consistent with pulmonary metastases. Progression of right obturator lymph node (now 3.1 cm) and new left external iliac lymph node (1.8 cm). Progressive osseous metastases. Findings consistent with olaparib resistance and visceral disease progression (liver and lung metastases).

At age 71 years and 8 months after starting olaparib, the patient had repeat next-generation sequencing performed on a liver biopsy specimen to evaluate resistance mechanisms. Findings: BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) retained (VAF 82%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 55%), PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 loss (homozygous deletion, new from prior), AR amplification (copy number 12, newly acquired — AR pathway re-activation), AR ligand-binding domain mutation p.L702H (VAF 22%, known resistance mechanism to enzalutamide and potentially olaparib combination), MYC amplification (copy number 18, increased from prior), CDK4 amplification (copy number 8), CCND1 gain, PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 18%, newly detected), FOXA1 p.R265Q retained, SPOP p.F133V retained. No BRCA2 reversion mutations detected. TMB: 5 mutations/Mb (low). MSI: microsatellite stable.

At age 71 years and 8 months after starting olaparib, the patient had a CT-guided liver biopsy for histologic assessment and molecular profiling. Results: metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with mixed features. Adenocarcinoma confirmed (PSA positive, NKX3.1 positive). Focal neuroendocrine differentiation present (synaptophysin focally positive in 10-15% of tumor cells, chromogranin A focally positive) — suggesting early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC) transdifferentiation. AR expression retained (moderate, 2+). Ki-67 proliferation index 55% (high). BRCA2 loss by FISH confirmed.

At age 71 years and 9 months after starting olaparib, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and subsequent oncologist office assessment to discuss disease progression with visceral metastases and early neuroendocrine features. PSA: 18.4 ng/mL. Chromogranin A: 142 ng/mL (elevated). Neuron-specific enolase: 28 U/L (elevated). LFTs: AST 52 U/L, ALT 48 U/L (mildly elevated due to hepatic metastases). Hgb 8.9 g/dL (requiring supportive transfusion again). Performance status ECOG 2-3. The patient and family were counseled on the aggressive nature of the disease, treatment options including platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin/etoposide given neuroendocrine features) versus cabazitaxel versus best supportive care. The patient expressed preference to pursue one additional line of therapy with understanding of goals of care. Disease status: cancer present, progressive on olaparib with visceral (hepatic and pulmonary) metastases and emerging neuroendocrine differentiation. Olaparib discontinued.

At age 71 years and 9 months after starting olaparib (age approximately 72 years), the patient was initiated on carboplatin AUC 5 IV day 1 plus etoposide 100 mg/m2 IV days 1-3 every 21 days, targeting the treatment-emergent neuroendocrine component. ADT (leuprolide) was continued. Denosumab was continued. Palliative care was formally integrated into the treatment plan. Goals of care discussion documented — patient desires life-prolonging therapy with clear understanding that treatment is palliative in intent. Hospice discussion deferred at patient request.

documents.text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Attending Physician: [Attending Oncologist]
Department: Medical Oncology, Genitourinary Oncology Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for scheduled 8-week follow-up since initiating Xtandi. He comes in today for PSA check, toxicity assessment, and ongoing disease management review.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old retired steelworker of Eastern European descent with a well-established history of metastatic prostate cancer, initially diagnosed approximately three years ago at age 68. At diagnosis, he presented with a PSA of 187 ng/mL and was found to have a Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5 prostate adenocarcinoma with bilateral seminal vesicle involvement, a right obturator lymph node metastasis (1.2 cm), and multiple bone metastases involving the lumbar spine at L3 and L4, the right iliac wing, and the right femoral neck. Germline testing at that time confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with hereditary BRCA2 syndrome (his family history is notable for a brother with pancreatic cancer and a sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer).

He was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy with Lupron acetate plus abi/prednisone for metastatic hormone-sensitive disease, in line with LATITUDE and STAMPEDE intensification data. He had an excellent initial biochemical response — PSA nadir reached 0.8 ng/mL at approximately six months — and his bone scan at 18 months showed sclerotic changes consistent with treatment response at the known sites, with no new lesions. The right obturator lymph node decreased to 0.7 cm at that reassessment.

Approximately two years into his treatment course, serial PSA measurements began to rise — from a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL to 4.7 ng/mL across three consecutive measurements spaced four weeks apart — while testosterone remained firmly suppressed below 20 ng/dL. This fulfilled PCWG3 biochemical criteria for PSA progression, and the clinical diagnosis of castration-resistant prostate cancer was formally established. He also endorsed worsening low back pain at the L3-L4 level at that juncture, rated 4/10 in intensity and managed with scheduled acetaminophen.

Subsequent PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) at the time of CRPC diagnosis showed worrisome interval change: increased PSMA avidity at the known L3 and L4 vertebral lesions, plus four new PSMA-avid sites — a T10 vertebral body lesion, a right posterior 7th rib lesion, a left posterior 9th rib lesion, and a right sacral ala lesion — as well as interval enlargement of the right obturator lymph node to 1.4 cm with increased avidity. No visceral metastases were identified, and there were no PSMA-negative lesions of concern. Given this picture of bone-dominant mCRPC after Zytiga, Xtandi 160 mg orally once daily was initiated approximately eight weeks ago. leup was continued for ongoing castrate testosterone maintenance. Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every four weeks was also added at that visit for bone health protection, along with calcium and vitamin D supplementation.

Mr. Kowalski returns today reporting that his back pain has improved meaningfully since starting enza — he rates it approximately 2/10 and has been able to reduce his acetaminophen use to as-needed rather than scheduled dosing. He notes ongoing fatigue, which he describes as moderate, making it difficult to complete his usual household activities and short neighborhood walks without stopping to rest. He also endorses what he characterizes as "brain fog" — mild word-finding difficulty and some slowing of his thinking — which he attributes to the new medication. He denies any falls, seizures, loss of consciousness, focal neurological deficits, or chest pain. He has not noted any lower extremity weakness or saddle anesthesia. He denies dysuria, hematuria, or new urinary obstruction symptoms. He does report persistent hot flashes, approximately five to six per day, which he attributes to the ongoing Lupron. He has otherwise been able to remain at home with his wife and maintain most instrumental activities of daily living with some assistance.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Fatigue (moderate, Grade 2), mild cognitive slowing/word-finding difficulty (Grade 1), hot flashes (Grade 1, ADT-related), mild low back discomfort (markedly improved from prior, now Grade 1).
Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No nausea, vomiting, or significant appetite loss. No diarrhea or constipation. No dyspnea at rest or orthopnea. No palpitations. No new lower extremity edema. No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary retention. No focal neurological symptoms, weakness, or paresthesias. No falls or seizures since starting enza. No new bone pain other than improving back pain. No right upper quadrant discomfort or jaundice.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, with bone (L3, L4, T10, bilateral ribs, sacrum, right femoral neck/iliac wing) and lymph node (right obturator) metastases — currently on enza + Lupron + denosumab
2. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) — hereditary BRCA2 syndrome
3. Hypertension — managed with amlodipine and previously required medication adjustment during Zytiga therapy
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled, on oral agents
5. Hyperlipidemia — on statin

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired steelworker, married, lives with his wife in the community. Eastern European descent. Lifelong non-smoker. No alcohol or illicit drug use. Two adult children who are supportive and accompany him to some appointments. Functional baseline prior to cancer diagnosis was ECOG 0 with full independent activity.

FAMILY HISTORY
Brother: pancreatic cancer (deceased)
Sister: breast cancer, BRCA2-associated
Family history strongly consistent with hereditary BRCA2 syndrome (genetic counseling completed at time of diagnosis).

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. enza (Xtandi) 160 mg orally once daily — initiated approximately 8 weeks ago for mCRPC
2. leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg intramuscular every 3 months — ongoing ADT, last injection given at CRPC transition visit
3. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks — initiated at CRPC transition, bone protection
4. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally twice daily — bone support
5. Vitamin D3 1000 IU orally daily — bone support
6. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily — hypertension
7. Lisinopril 20 mg orally daily — hypertension (dose adjusted during prior Zytiga therapy)
8. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily — type 2 diabetes
9. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally nightly — hyperlipidemia
10. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally as needed (now using less frequently) — pain management

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/82 mmHg (adequate, within acceptable range on current antihypertensives), heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 98.2°F (36.8°C), oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 181 lbs (down approximately 2 lbs from last visit — patient attributes to mild appetite change), BMI 26.8 kg/m².

General: Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male who appears mildly fatigued but is alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. He is well-groomed and conversational, though he pauses occasionally during conversation — consistent with his reported mild cognitive slowing.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Sclera anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No oral lesions.

Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly, no JVD.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ throughout. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or rales. Breathing unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds normoactive. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. No right upper quadrant tenderness.

Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness to deep palpation over the lower lumbar spine (L3-L4 region), unchanged or improved from prior visit. No midline spine tenderness at thoracic levels. No new focal bone tenderness at ribs or right hip today, though he reports mild right hip ache at baseline. Full range of motion at knees and hips without major limitation. Gait is steady without assistive device, though somewhat slow and deliberate.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II through XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No evidence of myelopathy. No ataxia on finger-nose testing. Romberg negative.

Skin: No rash, jaundice, or significant pallor. No ecchymoses suggesting coagulopathy.

Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy on examination.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
PSA: 1.9 ng/mL (down from 4.7 ng/mL at enza initiation — a 59% decline from baseline; nadir pending further monitoring)
Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL (castrate range confirmed, maintained on Lupron)
Complete Blood Count:
  - Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, likely multifactorial: ADT-related, chronic disease; stable from last check)
  - Hematocrit: 34.1%
  - White blood cell count: 5.8 K/uL (within normal limits)
  - Platelet count: 212 K/uL (within normal limits)
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L, BUN: 18 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~68 mL/min/1.73m², stable CKD stage 2)
  - AST: 28 U/L, ALT: 22 U/L, Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase: 118 U/L (mildly elevated, bone metastasis-related, stable from prior)
  - Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
  - Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting, acceptable diabetic control)
Lipid panel: LDL 78 mg/dL, HDL 42 mg/dL, Triglycerides 138 mg/dL (acceptable on atorvastatin)
HbA1c (recent): 7.1% (well-controlled diabetes)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
No new imaging obtained at this visit. Prior PSMA PET/CT (performed at time of CRPC diagnosis, approximately 8 weeks ago) is the most recent cross-sectional/functional imaging study. That scan demonstrated multiple PSMA-avid bone lesions (L3, L4, T10, bilateral posterior ribs, right sacral ala, right femoral neck region) and an enlarged right obturator lymph node at 1.4 cm. No visceral metastases were identified at that time. Restaging imaging will be planned at approximately the 5-6 month mark on enza per standard practice. The meaningful PSA decline observed today is reassuring as an early indicator of biochemical response.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) — early biochemical response to Xtandi.
Mr. Kowalski has achieved a significant early PSA response on Xtandi, with PSA declining from 4.7 ng/mL at baseline to 1.9 ng/mL today — a 59% reduction at the 8-week mark. Testosterone remains in the castrate range. This is an encouraging early response. His back pain has also improved considerably, consistent with a meaningful anti-tumor effect at the bone disease sites. We will continue Xtandi at the current dose of 160 mg daily. No dose adjustment is warranted at this time. Lupron injections will be maintained on schedule for continuous testosterone suppression. Repeat PSA and labs will be obtained in approximately 6-8 weeks. Formal restaging with bone scan and CT (versus PSMA PET/CT) will be planned at approximately 5-6 months to objectively assess radiographic response.

I discussed with Mr. Kowalski that while early data are promising, the CRPC disease state often evolves resistance over time. Given his germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant with confirmed somatic second hit (loss of heterozygosity on prior tissue NGS), Lynparza (Lynparza) is a well-established next-line option upon Xtandi progression, based on the PROfound trial. This has been discussed as part of our longitudinal planning and the patient is aware. No PARP inhibitor is indicated at present while enza is controlling disease.

2. enza-related fatigue — Grade 2, manageable.
The patient's Grade 2 fatigue is a well-recognized and expected adverse effect of Xtandi. It is somewhat compounded by ongoing ADT-related fatigue and mild anemia. At this time, it does not warrant dose reduction given the excellent early PSA response and the patient's overall ECOG 1 performance status. I encouraged him to engage with a physical therapy program — light aerobic exercise (walking, low-impact activity) is one of the most evidence-based interventions for cancer-related fatigue and may also reduce the risk of falls and sarcopenia in the context of ADT-related muscle loss. A formal PT referral was placed today.

3. Xtandi-related cognitive effects — Grade 1, monitoring.
Mild cognitive slowing is a recognized CNS-related adverse effect of Xtandi, particularly in older patients. His symptoms are currently Grade 1 (subjective slowing, mild word-finding, no impact on major ADLs). I counseled him to avoid driving if he feels cognitively impaired at any point and to notify us of any escalation. Formal neuropsychological evaluation is not yet indicated given the mild and expected nature of his symptoms. His wife was present for this discussion and will help monitor.

4. Fall risk — counseling and safety.
Although Mr. Kowalski has not fallen since starting enza, his age (71 years), ongoing ADT (which contributes to muscle weakness, reduced proprioception, and postural hypotension), fatigue, and mild cognitive effects all contribute to elevated fall risk. We discussed fall prevention measures: ensuring the home environment is clear of trip hazards, adequate lighting at night, use of handrails on stairs, and avoidance of activities requiring quick reflexes in the setting of any cognitive slowing. Physical therapy referral above will also address balance and gait safety.

5. Bone metastasis management — denosumab continued.
Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks is being continued for bone health protection in the setting of extensive osseous metastatic disease. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation ongoing. I reminded the patient of the importance of dental hygiene and to notify us before any invasive dental procedures given the rare but serious risk of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ) with denosumab. He has not had any dental work recently and was advised to have a routine dental cleaning but to hold off on any tooth extractions without discussion with our office first. He reported no jaw pain, loose teeth, or oral lesions today.

6. Hypertension — acceptable blood pressure control.
Blood pressure today at 138/82 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril 20 mg. This is acceptable. We will continue current antihypertensive regimen. Note that blood pressure management was more challenging during the prior Zytiga-containing regimen; enza does not carry the same mineralocorticoid excess effect, and blood pressure has remained more stable since the transition.

7. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled.
Fasting glucose 112 mg/dL, HbA1c 7.1%. Metformin continued. No adjustment needed. Renal function stable at eGFR ~68 mL/min, adequate for metformin use at current dose.

8. Mild anemia — monitoring.
Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, consistent with mild chronic anemia in the context of ongoing ADT and metastatic malignancy. No evidence of active bleeding or hemolysis. Iron stores not formally checked today — will reassess at next visit. No transfusion indication at this time. Will monitor CBC at each follow-up.

9. Goals of care — ongoing longitudinal discussion.

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for restaging assessment following approximately 5 months of treatment with enzalutamide. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is well known to our GU oncology service. To briefly summarize his history: he was initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago at age 68 with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 4+5 = 9 (Grade Group 5), with bone metastases to L3, L4, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck, plus a single right obturator lymph node metastasis. He underwent germline testing which identified a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), and somatic NGS revealed biallelic BRCA2 loss, TP53 p.R248W, PTEN deletion, RB1 heterozygous deletion, and MYC gain, among other alterations. He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide plus abiraterone/prednisone (LATITUDE/STAMPEDE intensification), achieving a PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL with radiographic improvement on restaging imaging at 18 months.

Given CRPC with bone-dominant disease, prior AR pathway intensification with abiraterone, intact performance status (ECOG 1), and no visceral crisis, he was transitioned to enzalutamide 160 mg daily approximately 5 months ago. Leuprolide was continued for ongoing castration. Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks was added at that time for bone protection. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC) — RESPONDING TO ENZALUTAMIDE

Mr. Kowalski is doing well on enzalutamide 5 months into treatment. He has achieved a PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL, representing an 87% reduction from his baseline at enzalutamide initiation (4.7 ng/mL). ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER — PROGRESSION ON ENZALUTAMIDE

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with mCRPC, Gleason 9, Grade Group 5, with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (biallelic loss confirmed on prior somatic testing), who has experienced clear biochemical progression on enzalutamide. His PSA has risen from a nadir of 0.6 ng/mL to 8.3 ng/mL over three consecutive monthly measurements — a rise of 7.7 ng/mL and greater than a 25% increase above nadir, meeting standard PCWG3-aligned PSA progression criteria. This is in the setting of confirmed castrate testosterone levels, so cross-resistance within the AR-signaling pathway is likely contributing. The new right hip pain in the context of a known right femoral neck osseous metastasis raises concern for progression at that site, and the worsening lumbar back pain similarly suggests osseous disease activity. While I cannot confirm radiographic progression without imaging, the clinical picture is highly consistent with progression, and management will proceed accordingly.

Plan:
- Enzalutamide will be continued at the current dose of 160 mg daily until restaging imaging is completed, per standard practice to avoid a potential flare.
- Urgent restaging PSMA PET/CT ordered. Given this patient's clinical trajectory and the PSMA-avid disease documented on prior PET at the time of CRPC diagnosis, PSMA PET/CT is the most sensitive modality and will help characterize extent of osseous progression, nodal burden, and — critically — whether any visceral metastases have emerged given his high-risk disease features (Gleason 9, BRCA2 loss, TP53 mutation, PTEN loss, RB1 loss on prior genomic profiling).
- Right hip dedicated MRI ordered in parallel given the new right hip pain over a known right femoral neck lesion. I am concerned about structural integrity of the proximal right femur. Orthopedic oncology referral placed contingently if the MRI reveals cortical erosion or at-risk fracture morphology — prophylactic fixation may be required.
- Results to be reviewed at a planned follow-up visit or sooner by telephone if urgent findings are identified.
- Will plan to formally discontinue enzalutamide and transition to next-line therapy at the follow-up visit after imaging review.

Next-line therapy consideration — PARP inhibitor (olaparib):
Given this patient's germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) with confirmed somatic second hit (biallelic loss) documented on his prior somatic NGS at diagnosis and confirmed on bone biopsy at the time of initial CRPC, he is an excellent candidate for olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg twice daily. This is supported by the PROfound trial data demonstrating significant radiographic progression-free survival benefit and overall survival benefit in BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC. The eligibility is clear and the genomic rationale is compelling. We will plan to initiate olaparib following imaging confirmation of progression. ADT will be maintained throughout per standard of care.

I will also consider whether a repeat biopsy of a progressive lesion is warranted at the time of progression to evaluate for treatment resistance mechanisms and any phenotypic changes (e.g., neuroendocrine transdifferentiation), which can occur with sequential AR pathway inhibitor exposure, particularly in patients with RB1 loss.

Baseline hematologic considerations for olaparib: Hemoglobin currently 11.1 g/dL — mildly decreased, which is concerning given the known myelosuppressive potential of olaparib. MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

Sections through the decalcified core needle biopsy material demonstrate markedly hypercellular bone marrow space virtually entirely replaced by infiltrating carcinoma. The neoplastic cells form poorly differentiated infiltrative glands (Gleason pattern 4) with cribriform architecture, as well as sheets and cords of individual cells with marked nuclear pleomorphism (Gleason pattern 5). The individual tumor cells have large vesicular nuclei with prominent macronucleoli, moderate-to-scant cytoplasm, and frequent mitotic figures including atypical forms. Focal comedo-type necrosis is identified within cribriform gland structures, occupying approximately 5–8% of tumor surface area. The surrounding bony trabeculae are focally eroded and remodeled, with reactive fibrosis and scattered osteoclast-type giant cells at the tumor-bone interface. Hematopoietic elements are markedly reduced and largely replaced by tumor.

There is no convincing morphologic evidence of small cell or large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma. No rosette formation, salt-and-pepper chromatin, or neuroendocrine organoid architecture is identified on hematoxylin-and-eosin sections. There are no features of squamous or sarcomatoid differentiation.

Ki-67 proliferation index (MIB-1 clone): Estimated at approximately 35% of viable tumor nuclei (hot-spot analysis).

COMMENT

The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings confirm metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason grade group 5, in this CT-guided right iliac crest biopsy specimen. The tumor demonstrates high-grade histologic features including cribriform architecture with comedo necrosis, pattern 5 growth, and a high Ki-67 proliferation index (35%). Importantly, IHC markers of neuroendocrine differentiation are entirely negative on this specimen, including synaptophysin, chromogranin A, CD56, and INSM1, indicating that frank neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer) is not present at this biopsy site at this time.

The diffuse androgen receptor (AR) positivity by IHC is noted; this does not exclude the possibility of AR amplification or AR mutation at the DNA/genomic level (which may not be detectable by protein IHC alone), and the pending NGS panel results are expected to provide additional information regarding AR pathway alterations.

Biallelic BRCA2 loss by FISH, combined with the known germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, confirms eligibility criteria consistent with published PROfound trial inclusion criteria for PARP inhibitor therapy in mCRPC.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with heavily pre-treated mCRPC — having progressed through ADT plus abi in the mHSPC setting, followed by Xtandi in the CRPC setting — now 6 weeks into PARP inhibitor therapy with Lynparza 300 mg BID, selected on the basis of his confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss (germline c.5946delT pathogenic variant with somatic second hit by LOH demonstrated on both NGS and FISH on bone biopsy).

1. ola RESPONSE — ENCOURAGING EARLY BIOCHEMICAL DECLINE
PSA has fallen from 8.3 ng/mL to 4.1 ng/mL, a 51% decline at 6 weeks. This is consistent with the magnitude and kinetics of PSA responses seen in BRCA2-mutated patients in PROfound and related trials, where patients with BRCA1/2 alterations demonstrated the most robust benefits from ola. This is promising and we will continue full-dose Lynparza 300 mg BID. The patient and family were encouraged by this result. Right hip pain has improved, which may also reflect early tumor response at the femoral metastasis, though this is difficult to attribute with certainty at this early stage.

2. ANEMIA — EARLY Lynparza-RELATED MYELOSUPPRESSION
Hemoglobin has dropped from 11.8 g/dL at baseline to 10.2 g/dL today — a decline of 1.6 g/dL over 6 weeks. This is a Grade 1 anemia by CTCAE criteria at this hemoglobin level, though it warrants close monitoring. Anemia is one of the most common and clinically significant adverse effects of Lynparza, occurring in approximately 20-25% of patients in pivotal trials, often reaching Grade 3 severity over time. The patient's baseline anemia risk is further compounded by his chronic disease state, prior androgen deprivation, and prior ADT-related erythropoietin suppression.
  - Reticulocyte count is pending; will review.
  - Iron studies will be sent today to establish baseline and identify any treatable contributing iron deficiency.
  - Plan: CBC to be repeated in 3 weeks and again at the 3-month mark. No dose modification at this time — anemia is Grade 1 and patient is asymptomatic from a hematologic standpoint. Will counsel the patient to report worsening dyspnea, chest pain, lightheadedness, or palpitations immediately.
  - Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not recommended given active malignancy context and associated thromboembolic risk.

3. NAUSEA AND FATIGUE — MANAGEABLE TOXICITY
Gastrointestinal side effects (nausea) have improved substantially with scheduled/PRN ondansetron and taking Lynparza with food. Ongoing nausea Grade 1, no dose modification needed. Fatigue is Grade 2, which is significant but not uncommon in the context of ola plus the cumulative burden of prolonged ADT, progressive bone disease, and underlying anemia. No dose reduction is warranted at this time.
  - Discussed fatigue management strategies: pacing activity, prioritizing rest, light physical activity as tolerated.
  - Physical therapy referral from prior fall workup on Xtandi remains active — encouraged him to attend scheduled sessions.

4. BONE HEALTH AND PAIN MANAGEMENT
Denosumab 120 mg SC monthly is ongoing. Next dose due in approximately 2 weeks — reminder given to patient/wife. Dental hygiene reviewed — no planned dental procedures in the near future, which is appropriate given denosumab use and osteonecrosis of the jaw risk (though ONJ risk over years of use is notable and patient has been counseled previously). Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continued.
Right hip pain (right femoral head/neck lesion) is improving and currently Grade 1. No concern for impending pathologic fracture at this time, but if pain worsens or function declines we would obtain orthopedic surgery and/or radiation oncology consultation for prophylactic fixation versus palliative radiation. Acetaminophen continued PRN.

5. ONGOING ADT
leup acetate continued at 22.5 mg IM every 3 months — next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Testosterone confirmed castrate at < 20 ng/dL — confirming continued AR-axis suppression is maintained alongside ola therapy. Continuation of ADT alongside PARP inhibitor therapy is standard practice in mCRPC per current guidelines and trial protocols.

6. COMORBIDITY MANAGEMENT
Hypertension: BP 138/82 today — acceptable control. Amlodipine and lisinopril continued. Will monitor closely.
Type 2 diabetes: Patient reports good dietary compliance. Metformin continued. Will defer detailed DM management to PCP; HbA1c check recommended at next PCP visit.
Hyperlipidemia: Atorvastatin continued.

7. GOALS OF CARE AND PATIENT EDUCATION
The early PSA response was reviewed with the patient and his wife, who were encouraged. We discussed that while this is a promising early signal, our goals of therapy remain palliative — focused on disease control, quality of life, and prolonging meaningful function. We discussed the typical trajectory of PARP inhibitor therapy in BRCA2-mutated mCRPC — response durations in PROfound ranged from months to over a year for excellent responders with BRCA1/2 mutations, but eventual resistance is anticipated. We have noted that his NGS profile at initial biopsy already showed co-occurring TP53 mutation, PTEN deletion, and RB1 heterozygous loss — markers of genomic complexity that may be relevant to response duration and future resistance.
The patient reaffirmed his desire to continue active treatment and his understanding that treatment is palliative in intent. He deferred hospice/palliative care integration discussion for now, but this will be revisited at each visit.

- Labs at next visit: CBC with differential, CMP, PSA, testosterone, iron studies review.
- Formal restaging imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or bone scan) to be scheduled at the 5-month mark on ola.
- Continue ola 300 mg PO BID at this time — no dose modification.
- Patient instructed to call the oncology triage line promptly if he develops worsening shortness of breath, fever, significant worsening of bone pain, or any new symptoms.

DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSTIC NOTE:
The histomorphology is diagnostic of high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma. The combination of poorly formed glands (Gleason pattern 4) and single-cell infiltration/comedonecrosis (Gleason pattern 5) confers Gleason score 9, the highest-risk category. The extensive bilateral core involvement (10/12), presence of perineural invasion, high Ki-67 index, and markedly elevated clinical PSA (187 ng/mL) are all consistent with a high-volume, aggressive disease process. Correlation with clinical staging is strongly advised.

The IHC panel confirms prostatic origin (PSA+, NKX3.1+, AR strongly+) and supports invasive carcinoma (absent basal cell layer). The wild-type p53 IHC pattern (note: molecular correlation pending) and lack of neuroendocrine differentiation at this time are noteworthy. Clinical and molecular follow-up recommended.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is a retired steelworker with a well-characterized history of high-risk metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer diagnosed approximately 3 years ago, at that time presenting with a PSA of 187 ng/mL, Gleason 9 (4+5) adenocarcinoma, and metastatic disease involving the lumbar spine (L3, L4), right iliac wing, right femoral neck, and a right obturator lymph node. He harbors a germline BRCA2 pathogenic frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome — a finding that has now directly informed his current therapeutic strategy.

He was initially managed with androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) using leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily with prednisone, with an excellent initial PSA response to nadir of 0.8 ng/mL. He progressed biochemically and radiographically approximately 24 months later (CRPC designation), at which point abiraterone was discontinued and he was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily. He achieved a PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL on enzalutamide, with stable disease on bone scan and nodal response noted on CT. However, approximately 6 months into enzalutamide, he again progressed — PSA rose to 8.3 ng/mL, imaging on PSMA PET/CT confirmed marked osseous progression with multiple new bone metastases throughout the thoracic and lumbar spine, bilateral iliac crests, ribs, sternum, and a growing right obturator lymph node — and enzalutamide was discontinued.

Given his confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss (germline mutation plus somatic second hit with LOH confirmed on prior biopsy, and confirmed again on CT-guided bone biopsy of the right iliac crest immediately prior to this line of therapy), olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID was initiated approximately 4 months ago per FDA-approved indication in BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC. Leuprolide was continued for ongoing castrate testosterone suppression. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks was continued for bone protection.

At his 6-week follow-up on olaparib, PSA had declined to 4.1 ng/mL — a 51% reduction from baseline — consistent with a meaningful early biochemical response. At that time, Hgb had already begun to fall to 10.2 g/dL. He was managed with watchful waiting and instructed to monitor for symptoms.

Approximately 3 months into olaparib, he developed symptomatic Grade 3 anemia with Hgb of 7.8 g/dL, presenting with dyspnea on exertion and worsening fatigue. Reticulocyte count was low at 1.1%, consistent with hypoproliferative anemia from olaparib-related myelosuppression. Contributing factors included borderline iron deficiency (ferritin 68 ng/mL, TIBC 320, serum iron 45). B12 and folate were normal. Hemolysis workup was negative. He received 2 units of pRBCs with post-transfusion Hgb rise to 10.1 g/dL. IV iron was administered as ferric carboxymaltose 750 mg. Olaparib was dose-reduced to 200 mg BID per FDA labeling guidance for hematologic toxicity. He was not started on an ESA given the oncologic context and thromboembolic risk considerations.

He presents today for assessment of anemia trajectory, tolerability of olaparib at the reduced dose, and overall disease status.

He reports that since the dose reduction and transfusion approximately 3 weeks ago, he has felt "considerably better." Fatigue has improved significantly — he rates it as mild (Grade 1) and is now able to manage light household activities and walk around the neighborhood. Nausea, which had been a persistent Grade 1 complaint on the higher dose, has since resolved. He is tolerating oral intake without difficulty. He denies dyspnea at rest or with minimal exertion. He reports no chest pain, palpitations, or lightheadedness.

Musculoskeletal: Bone pain is minimal today. He notes only mild residual aching in the lower back, which he rates 2/10 and manages with acetaminophen 650 mg TID as needed. The right hip discomfort that had been present at the time of CRPC progression has substantially improved. He denies any new focal bone pain or radicular symptoms.

Neurologically, he denies any symptoms suggestive of spinal cord compression — no new weakness in the lower extremities, no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no saddle anesthesia. MULTIDISCIPLINARY ONCOLOGY OFFICE VISIT NOTE
Genitourinary Medical Oncology

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with known metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for oncologist office assessment following multidisciplinary tumor board (MTB) review of his case. He was referred to MTB given confirmed visceral disease progression on olaparib, newly identified hepatic and pulmonary metastases, histologic evidence of early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine differentiation, and a rising PSA. The purpose of today's visit is to discuss MTB recommendations, review all available data, and determine a next line of treatment in the setting of visceral crisis and evolving disease biology.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Mr. Kowalski is well known to this service. Briefly, he was diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), presenting with a PSA of 187 ng/mL, Gleason 9 (4+5) disease, bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, a right obturator lymph node metastasis, and bone metastases to L3-L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck. Germline testing at diagnosis revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with a hereditary cancer predisposition given his family history (brother with pancreatic cancer, sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer). Somatic NGS at that time showed biallelic BRCA2 inactivation (VAF 78%), TP53 p.R248W, PTEN homozygous deletion, and RB1 heterozygous deletion — a highly unfavorable genomic profile.

He was initially treated with ADT (leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM q3 months) plus abiraterone/prednisone (Zytiga 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID) per LATITUDE/STAMPEDE intensification data. He had an excellent initial response, with PSA nadiring at 0.8 ng/mL, before developing biochemical and radiographic castration resistance at roughly 24 months — meeting PCWG3 biochemical progression criteria, with a rising PSA from 0.8 to 4.7 ng/mL and new PSMA-avid bone lesions on Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT (new lesions at T10, right posterior 7th rib, left posterior 9th rib, and right sacral ala).

He then received enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily, again with a favorable initial biochemical response (PSA nadir 0.6 ng/mL), before progressing at approximately 6 months with a rising PSA (8.3 ng/mL) and extensive new osseous disease on repeat PSMA PET/CT — new lesions at bilateral iliac crests, L1-L2, sternum, multiple new rib lesions, and enlargement of the right obturator node to 2.1 cm. A CT-guided bone biopsy of a right iliac crest lesion at that time confirmed metastatic Gleason GG5 adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine transdifferentiation, with AR positivity and BRCA2 homozygous deletion by FISH.

Given his biallelic BRCA2 loss, olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID was initiated as the third line of therapy, consistent with PROfound trial data. He demonstrated an initial PSA response (nadir 2.1 ng/mL), but his course was complicated by recurrent Grade 3 anemia requiring two separate pRBC transfusions (first at 3 months, second at 6 months), necessitating a dose reduction to 200 mg BID. Despite the dose reduction, he continued to have PSA response (2.8 ng/mL at 4 months) and imaging showed partial response at 5 months, with the right obturator node shrinking to 1.2 cm and improving bone scan signal at multiple sites.

However, approximately 8 months after starting olaparib, he developed a rapidly rising PSA (from nadir 2.1 to 14.6 ng/mL), new right upper quadrant discomfort, and a 6-lb unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks. Physical examination at that visit noted mild hepatomegaly. Urgent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrated visceral disease spread: new hepatic metastases in the right (3 lesions, largest 2.8 cm) and left (1 lesion, 1.4 cm) hepatic lobes, new pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe (0.9 cm) and left upper lobe (0.7 cm), enlargement of the right obturator lymph node (3.1 cm), new left external iliac lymph node (1.8 cm), and progressive osseous disease. A CT-guided liver biopsy was performed and pathology revealed metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with focal neuroendocrine differentiation (synaptophysin positive in 10-15% of cells, chromogranin A focally positive), a Ki-67 proliferation index of 55%, and retained AR expression (moderate, 2+) — histologic findings consistent with early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). Repeat somatic NGS on the liver biopsy showed AR amplification (copy number 12, newly acquired), an AR ligand-binding domain mutation (p.L702H, VAF 22%), newly acquired RB1 homozygous deletion, MYC amplification (copy number 18, increased from copy number 6 at diagnosis), PIK3CA p.H1047R (VAF 18%, newly detected), and no BRCA2 reversion mutations — indicating both AR pathway reactivation and frank clonal evolution. BRCA2 frameshift was retained.

The case was presented to the GU multidisciplinary tumor board, which convened this week. MTB attendees included GU medical oncology, radiation oncology, urology, pathology, radiology, palliative care, and genomics. MTB recommendation: systemic platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin/etoposide was felt to be the most appropriate next step given the histologic evidence of neuroendocrine differentiation, aggressive disease biology, and visceral spread. Cabazitaxel was discussed as an alternative for the pure adenocarcinoma component but felt to be less optimal given the NE features and rapid visceral progression. Clinical trial enrollment was explored but no currently available trials matched his molecular profile and status at this center.

Mr. Kowalski is accompanied today by his daughter. He reports continued right upper quadrant discomfort, moderate fatigue, and decreased appetite. He denies acute confusion, fevers, chills, or lower extremity swelling.

Extensive and substantive discussion held today with Mr. Kowalski and his daughter regarding the trajectory of his illness. I explained in direct but compassionate terms that his cancer has developed new spread to the liver and lungs, has features of a more aggressive subtype (neuroendocrine), and has exhausted several prior therapies. I also explained that carboplatin/etoposide is the recommended next treatment, with the dual goal of controlling the NE component and managing his visceral disease burden. I was honest that this remains a palliative treatment approach.

Mr. Kowalski articulated a clear understanding of the palliative intent of further therapy. He expressed that he wishes to pursue one additional line of treatment while maintaining quality of life, and specifically deferred hospice enrollment at this time. His goals are to maintain independence for as long as possible and to avoid prolonged hospitalization. He confirmed full code status at this time but asked that we revisit code status discussions at a future visit as the clinical situation evolves.

Palliative care is formally integrated into the treatment plan — palliative care attending was present at the tumor board and will be co-managing Mr. Kowalski throughout the chemotherapy course.

  - Palliative care follow-up to be scheduled in 1-2 weeks
  - Pain management: current regimen (scheduled acetaminophen + PRN oxycodone) appears adequate; will reassess pain at each visit; consider pain management/palliative care escalation if needed
  - Nutritional decline: dietitian referral placed; consider appetite stimulant (megestrol or mirtazapine) given 6-lb unintentional weight loss — will defer to dietitian input and palliative care recommendation
  - Social work referral placed for caregiver support and advance care planning assistance
  - Home health nursing evaluation to be arranged for support between infusion visits

*Problem 4: Hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus — stable*
BP 132/80 today, adequately controlled on amlodipine 10 mg. No adjustment needed. Metformin continued for diabetes; glucose 112 mg/dL fasting, HbA1c 6.8% at last check. Note: dexamethasone (part of anti-emetic regimen) may transiently worsen glycemic control; counsel patient to monitor glucose around chemotherapy days and contact PCP if glucose >250 mg/dL.

CLINICAL INDICATION:
71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, with known germline and somatic biallelic BRCA2 loss. Prior therapies include androgen deprivation therapy (leuprolide), abiraterone/prednisone (discontinued at CRPC transition), enzalutamide (discontinued at radiographic progression), and olaparib 200 mg twice daily (dose-reduced from 300 mg BID due to Grade 3 anemia requiring packed red blood cell transfusions). Patient now presents with rising PSA (14.6 ng/mL, up from nadir of 2.1 ng/mL), new right upper quadrant discomfort, unintentional weight loss of approximately 6 lbs over 6 weeks, and newly noted hepatomegaly on physical examination. Urgent cross-sectional restaging requested by medical oncology to evaluate for visceral disease progression.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350) with scanning in the portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformats were reviewed. Bone windows reviewed for osseous evaluation. No prior CT chest available for direct comparison; comparison made to prior restaging bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis from approximately 3 months prior.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. There are two new soft-tissue attenuation nodules in the right lower lobe, the larger measuring approximately 0.9 cm in greatest dimension, with a second adjacent smaller nodule of 0.5 cm. A separate nodule is identified in the left upper lobe measuring approximately 0.7 cm. These nodules have rounded, well-circumscribed morphology without calcification or satellite lesions, arising in a distribution atypical for infectious or inflammatory etiology. In the clinical context of widely metastatic prostate carcinoma with known progression, these pulmonary nodules are highly suspicious for hematogenous metastatic deposits. No associated pleural effusion, consolidation, or airspace disease. No endobronchial lesions are identified.

Pleurae: No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with mild atherosclerotic calcification consistent with patient's age.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: Sclerotic osseous lesions are again seen in the thoracic vertebral bodies (T10, previously identified) and in multiple posterior ribs bilaterally, consistent with known osseous metastatic disease. No new chest wall soft tissue mass. No new rib fractures identified, though the previously noted metastatic lesions at the right posterior 7th rib and left posterior 9th rib appear similar in extent.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is mildly enlarged, with the craniocaudal span measuring approximately 18.1 cm in the midclavicular line. There are multiple hypoattenuating lesions identified throughout the hepatic parenchyma that demonstrate peripheral rim enhancement on portal venous phase imaging, consistent with hypovascular metastatic deposits. The largest is located in the right hepatic lobe, segment VI/VII, measuring approximately 2.8 cm in greatest dimension. Two additional lesions are identified in the right lobe — one in segment V measuring approximately 1.7 cm, and a smaller lesion in segment VIII measuring approximately 1.1 cm. A fourth lesion is identified in the left hepatic lobe, segment III, measuring approximately 1.4 cm. These lesions were not present on prior imaging and represent new hepatic metastases. No evidence of biliary ductal dilatation. The hepatic veins and portal vein are patent. No perihepatic ascites.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is normal in appearance without cholelithiasis or wall thickening. The common bile duct measures 4 mm and is within normal limits.

Spleen: Normal in size (10.2 cm). No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreatic parenchyma is normal in attenuation and morphology. No pancreatic duct dilatation. No peripancreatic stranding or fluid collections. No focal pancreatic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: The adrenal glands are symmetric and unremarkable. No adrenal mass.

Kidneys and Ureters: The kidneys are normal in size and morphology with symmetric cortical enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No urolithiasis. No renal masses.

Stomach, Small Bowel, and Colon: Bowel loops are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness. No bowel obstruction. No mesenteric stranding. No free fluid.

Peritoneum: No ascites. No peritoneal implants identified.

Aorta and Major Vessels: The abdominal aorta demonstrates mild diffuse atherosclerotic calcification and is normal in caliber without aneurysm. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and inferior mesenteric artery origins appear patent.

---

PELVIS:

Lymph Nodes: There is marked enlargement of the right obturator lymph node, now measuring approximately 3.1 cm in short-axis diameter (previously 1.2 cm on prior CT at time of olaparib initiation, and 1.7 cm on most recent restaging scan approximately 3 months ago). This represents significant interval growth. A new soft-tissue density lymph node is identified in the left external iliac chain, measuring approximately 1.8 cm in short-axis diameter. This was not present on prior imaging. No new pelvic sidewall or presacral lymph nodes are identified. Mild bilateral inguinal lymph nodes are present, measuring up to 1.1 cm, which may reflect reactive changes; these will require attention on subsequent imaging.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and appears unremarkable in wall thickness. No bladder masses.

Prostate Bed: Post-biopsy changes in the prostate region. The prostate gland is not clearly delineated given prior disease involvement. No obvious large pelvic soft-tissue mass beyond the lymphadenopathy described.

Bony Pelvis and Osseous Structures: Multiple sclerotic and lytic osseous lesions are present throughout the pelvis, consistent with known metastatic prostate carcinoma. New lytic/sclerotic lesions are identified at the bilateral iliac crests, more pronounced on the right, appearing new or substantially more conspicuous compared to the prior bone scan findings. The previously identified lesion at the right sacral ala remains present. The right femoral head and neck lesion appears larger and more lytic in character compared to prior imaging, raising concern for increased disease burden at this site with potential structural compromise — the cortical integrity of the right femoral neck warrants close attention on dedicated radiographic evaluation. Lesions at L1, L2, L3, and L4 vertebral bodies are again noted, with the L1 and L2 lesions appearing more lytic compared to prior sclerotic change.

Sternal Lesion: A lytic lesion involving the mid-sternum, measuring approximately 1.5 cm, is again seen, consistent with previously identified metastatic focus.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NEW HEPATIC METASTASES: Four new hepatic lesions are identified as described, with the largest in the right hepatic lobe measuring 2.8 cm and an additional lesion in the left hepatic lobe measuring 1.4 cm. These findings are new compared to prior imaging and represent visceral progression. The liver is mildly enlarged.

2. NEW PULMONARY NODULES SUSPICIOUS FOR METASTATIC DISEASE: New nodules in the right lower lobe (dominant, 0.9 cm) and left upper lobe (0.7 cm) are identified, without a benign explanatory etiology, in a patient with known widely metastatic CRPC. These are highly suspicious for pulmonary metastatic deposits.

3. SIGNIFICANT LYMPH NODE PROGRESSION: The right obturator lymph node has grown substantially (now 3.1 cm, previously 1.2 cm at baseline olaparib initiation). A new left external iliac lymph node measuring 1.8 cm is newly identified, consistent with regional lymphatic disease spread.

4. PROGRESSIVE OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Worsening osseous disease is present with new/more conspicuous lesions at the bilateral iliac crests and more lytic change at L1-L2 vertebral bodies and the right femoral head/neck. The right femoral neck lesion warrants evaluation for structural integrity given its lytic character — dedicated plain radiograph and/or orthopedic surgery consultation may be of value for fracture risk stratification.

5. PREVIOUSLY IDENTIFIED OSSEOUS AND SOFT-TISSUE DISEASE: Known lesions at T10, multiple ribs, sacrum, and sternum are again identified without substantial change in overall extent, though these should be interpreted in the context of newly identified disease at other sites.

Overall, the findings are consistent with multi-site systemic progression on olaparib therapy, now including new visceral (hepatic and pulmonary) involvement, lymph node enlargement, and progressive osseous disease.

BRCA2 REVERSION MUTATION ANALYSIS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Clinical Question: Are secondary (reversion) mutations restoring BRCA2 open
reading frame present, which would predict resistance to PARP inhibitors?

Result: NO BRCA2 REVERSION MUTATIONS DETECTED

Analysis: The index BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22)
remains present at high VAF (82%), with no secondary intragenic mutations or
deletions detected that would restore the wild-type BRCA2 reading frame.
Extensive deep sequencing was performed across BRCA2 exons 11–27 (the mutation
locus and flanking sequence) at a minimum coverage of 1,000x to maximize
sensitivity for low-frequency reversion events. Additionally, no copy-neutral
reversion events (e.g., gene conversion) were detected by allele-specific
analysis.

Interpretation: Olaparib resistance in this patient does not appear to be
mediated by BRCA2 restoration. The dominant resistance mechanism is most
consistent with AR pathway re-engagement (AR amplification + AR p.L702H LBD
mutation), with concurrent loss of RB1 homozygosity potentially contributing
to phenotypic plasticity and neuroendocrine transdifferentiation.

PHARMACOGENOMIC SUMMARY — THERAPIES WITH POTENTIAL RELEVANCE

NOTE: The following is a genomic pharmacogenomic summary only. Therapeutic
decisions should be made by the treating oncologist in the context of full
clinical data, current standard of care, and available clinical trial
options. This report does not constitute a treatment recommendation.

PARP Inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib, talazoparib):
  - BRCA2 biallelic loss retained; no reversion mutations
  - Continued BRCA2 loss is a predictive biomarker for HRD/PARP inhibitor
    sensitivity; however, the dominant acquired resistance mechanisms (AR
    amplification, AR-V7, AR LBD mutation) suggest AR pathway escape as the
    primary mechanism of clinical progression rather than loss of HRD
  - AR pathway co-targeting may be required; lutetium-177 PSMA or other
    approaches also potentially applicable pending PSMA expression

AR Pathway Agents (enzalutamide, apalutamide, darolutamide, abiraterone):
  - AR amplification + AR p.L702H + AR-V7 collectively predict resistance
    to available AR pathway inhibitors as single agents
  - Novel AR degraders (ARV-110/bavdegalutamide) may retain activity
    against LBD-mutant AR; investigational

PI3K/AKT Pathway Inhibitors (ipatasertib, capivasertib, copanlisib):
  - PTEN loss + PIK3CA p.H1047R dual activation: strong preclinical rationale
    for PI3K/AKT inhibition; capivasertib (AKT inhibitor) in combination with
    abiraterone under evaluation in prostate cancer

CDK4/6 Inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib):
  - CDK4 amplification + CCND1 gain + RB1 loss: RB1 biallelic deletion
    predicts resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitors (RB1 protein loss abrogates
    the on-target mechanism); CDK4/6 inhibitors NOT predicted to be
    beneficial in this context

Platinum-Based Chemotherapy:
  - BRCA2 biallelic loss + RB1/TP53 co-alteration (neuroendocrine features):
    both HRD-related platinum sensitivity and standard NEPC treatment
    protocols support platinum-based regimens (e.g., carboplatin/etoposide)

Immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors, pembrolizumab):
  - MSS, TMB-low: immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy unlikely to provide
    benefit based on current biomarker criteria; CDK12 wild-type (no
    tandem duplicator phenotype)

INTERPRETATION AND CONCLUSIONS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This repeat comprehensive somatic genomic profiling of a hepatic metastasis
from a patient with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer reveals
a complex and evolving genomic landscape consistent with multi-mechanism
treatment resistance. The most clinically significant new findings are:

1. AR PATHWAY RE-ACTIVATION AS PRIMARY OLAPARIB RESISTANCE MECHANISM:
   Newly acquired AR amplification (high-level, CN 12) combined with a
   de novo AR ligand-binding domain mutation (p.L702H) and detectable
   AR-V7 splice variant mRNA collectively indicate that AR pathway
   re-engagement — rather than loss of BRCA2 function — is the dominant
   driver of progression on olaparib. This is consistent with emerging
   data demonstrating that AR signaling can provide a survival bypass
   to PARP-inhibitor-induced lethality in HRD-positive prostate cancer.
   The AR p.L702H mutation is of particular interest as it confers
   agonist activity to glucocorticoids (including prednisone, which this
   patient received concurrently during abiraterone therapy); it also
   reduces enzalutamide binding affinity.

2. BRCA2 BIALLELIC LOSS RETAINED WITHOUT REVERSION:
   The pathogenic BRCA2 germline frameshift mutation with somatic LOH is
   fully retained at high clonal fraction and no reversion mutations are
   present, indicating that homologous recombination deficiency persists.
   This is relevant for future platinum-based therapy (carboplatin) and
   potentially for re-challenge strategies or novel PARP-trapping approaches
   should AR co-targeting be employed.

3. COMPLETE RB1 LOSS AND NEUROENDOCRINE SUSCEPTIBILITY:
   The progression from heterozygous to homozygous RB1 deletion, in the
   context of concurrent TP53 gain-of-function mutation, constitutes the
   canonical genomic substrate for treatment-emergent neuroendocrine
   prostate cancer (t-NEPC). This finding is concordant with the
   histopathologic detection of focal neuroendocrine differentiation
   on the concurrent liver biopsy and with elevated serum chromogranin A
   and NSE, supporting a transition toward a mixed adenocarcinoma/NEPC
   phenotype and reinforcing the rationale for platinum/etoposide-based
   chemotherapy.

4. CONVERGING PI3K PATHWAY DYSREGULATION:
   The combination of pre-existing PTEN homozygous deletion and newly
   acquired PIK3CA p.H1047R creates dual convergent activation of the
   PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis. This pathway is an established co-driver of
   castration resistance and may represent a future therapeutic target,
   particularly in investigational combinations with hormonal therapies.

5. HIGH-LEVEL MYC AND CDK4 AMPLIFICATION:
   The dramatic increase in MYC copy number (CN 6 → 18) reflects
   progressive genomic instability and aggressive clonal evolution.
   MYC-driven transcriptional reprogramming may facilitate neuroendocrine
   lineage plasticity and metabolic adaptation to androgen-deprived
   conditions.

Overall, this genomic profile depicts a highly evolved, genomically
instable mCRPC with multi-pathway resistance, emerging neuroendocrine
transdifferentiation, and retained HRD — features that collectively
portend a challenging clinical course requiring multimodal therapeutic
strategy.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

All three cores are examined. Sections show fragments of liver parenchyma with near-total replacement by a malignant neoplasm. The tumor is composed predominantly of glands and nests of cells with moderate amounts of amphophilic cytoplasm, enlarged hyperchromatic nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and moderate nuclear pleomorphism. The glandular architecture is consistent with adenocarcinoma. Infiltrating tumor cells are present within portal tracts and sinusoidal spaces, and focal desmoplastic stromal reaction is present at the tumor-liver interface.

In addition to the predominant glandular component, a subpopulation of tumor cells with morphologic features suggestive of neuroendocrine differentiation is identified. These cells display a more monotonous appearance with round to oval nuclei, finely stippled ("salt and pepper") chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, and scant cytoplasm. They are arranged in small nests, trabeculae, and single-file patterns interspersed within and at the periphery of the glandular component, comprising an estimated 10-15% of the overall tumor cellularity. No well-formed organoid or rosette architecture diagnostic of small cell carcinoma is identified; the morphology is more consistent with focal/mixed neuroendocrine differentiation rather than overt small cell/large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma at this time.

Mitotic figures are numerous throughout the specimen (estimated >10 per 10 high-power fields in the most active areas). Apoptotic bodies are readily identified. No definitive lymphovascular invasion is identified within the sampled cores, though the biopsy nature limits evaluation. No perineural invasion is identified within the hepatic parenchyma.

The background non-neoplastic liver parenchyma is largely replaced by tumor. The residual hepatocytes show mild cytoplasmic vacuolization without significant steatosis, cholestasis, or bridging fibrosis.

COMMENT

This liver core needle biopsy demonstrates metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with focal features of treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). The combined adenocarcinoma and neuroendocrine components, retained PSA and NKX3.1 expression (confirming prostatic lineage), high Ki-67 proliferative index of 55%, RB1 loss, and the clinical context of prior AR-targeted therapy (abiraterone and enzalutamide) and PARP inhibitor therapy (olaparib) are collectively consistent with treatment-related lineage plasticity and early neuroendocrine transdifferentiation. The morphology does not fulfill criteria for overt small cell carcinoma at this time; the picture is best characterized as a mixed adenocarcinoma/focally neuroendocrine-differentiated phenotype.

Loss of RB1 expression in this setting is particularly notable, as RB1 loss in combination with TP53 mutation is well recognized as a critical driver of neuroendocrine lineage plasticity in prostate cancer. The retained AR expression (moderate, 2+) and weak PSA expression in the neuroendocrine component suggests an intermediate/transitional state rather than complete lineage switch.

Molecular profiling results are pending (see Ancillary Studies). These results will be reported in a separate addendum.
