HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old retired steelworker with a well-documented history of stage IV metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago. He carries a germline BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22), consistent with a hereditary breast/ovarian cancer syndrome pedigree (brother with pancreatic cancer, sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer). His initial Gleason 9 (4+5) adenocarcinoma presented with multifocal bone metastases (lumbar spine, right iliac wing, right femoral neck) and right obturator lymph node involvement, with a presenting PSA of 187 ng/mL.

He was initially treated with ADT (leuprolide) plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) with prednisone for mHSPC intensification, achieving a PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL and radiographic improvement. He developed CRPC approximately 2 years after diagnosis with PSA rise to 4.7 ng/mL on 3 consecutive measurements and new bone metastases on PSMA PET/CT. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily with an initial PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL, but progressed after approximately 6 months with rising PSA to 8.3 ng/mL and substantial new osseous metastases on repeat PSMA PET/CT. A confirmatory CT-guided right iliac crest biopsy at that time showed metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with biallelic BRCA2 loss confirmed by FISH, no neuroendocrine differentiation, Ki-67 35%.

Given his germline and somatic biallelic BRCA2 loss and PROfound trial data supporting olaparib (Lynparza) in this setting, he was initiated on olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily approximately 9 months ago. He had a meaningful initial response with PSA declining from 8.3 to a nadir of 2.1 ng/mL and radiographic improvement in osseous and nodal disease at 5-month restaging. His course was complicated by recurrent olaparib-related myelosuppression, requiring two separate packed red blood cell transfusions (the first at 3 months, the second at 6 months) and olaparib dose reduction to 200 mg twice daily following the first transfusion for Grade 3 anemia (Hgb nadir 7.6 g/dL). He also received IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) on two occasions and hematology was co-managing his anemia.

Approximately 3 weeks ago, he presented with new right upper quadrant discomfort, 6 lb unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks, and a PSA that had risen precipitously from the 2.1 nadir to 14.6 ng/mL. Physical exam revealed mild hepatomegaly. Urgent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast demonstrated new hepatic metastases (up to 2.8 cm in the right lobe, with a smaller left lobe lesion), new bilateral pulmonary nodules concerning for pulmonary metastases, progression of right obturator lymph node (3.1 cm) with new left external iliac adenopathy, and further progressive osseous disease. This represented clear visceral spread and olaparib resistance.

A CT-guided liver biopsy was performed and returned showing metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with focal neuroendocrine differentiation — synaptophysin focally positive in approximately 10-15% of tumor cells and chromogranin A focally positive — raising concern for treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC), a well-recognized phenomenon in this heavily pre-treated, AR-pathway-driven disease context. Ki-67 proliferation index was markedly elevated at 55%. PSA and NKX3.1 were retained, confirming the prostatic origin. AR expression was moderate (2+) rather than absent, suggesting an early or partial neuroendocrine transdifferentiation rather than fully dedifferentiated small cell NE carcinoma. Repeat somatic NGS on the liver biopsy specimen identified newly acquired AR amplification (copy number 12) and AR LBD mutation p.L702H, MYC amplification (now copy number 18, up from 6 at diagnosis), RB1 biallelic loss (new from prior), CDK4 amplification, CCND1 gain, and a newly detected PIK3CA p.H1047R mutation — a molecular landscape reflective of treatment-driven clonal evolution and aggressive disease biology. No BRCA2 reversion mutations were identified, confirming that PARP inhibitor resistance is not mediated by BRCA2 restoration in this case.

The case was reviewed at GU Multidisciplinary Tumor Board, attended by GU medical oncology, radiation oncology, urology, palliative care, and diagnostic radiology. The consensus recommendation was to discontinue olaparib and pursue platinum-based chemotherapy with carboplatin/etoposide, given the neuroendocrine features on histology, the heavily pre-treated state, and the established role of platinum doublets in t-NEPC. Cabazitaxel was also discussed but felt to be less optimal in the setting of emerging NE differentiation. Radiation for palliation of symptomatic osseous sites can be considered as needed. Formal integration of palliative care was strongly recommended.

Mr. Kowalski is here today with his daughter, who is his primary caregiver. He has been told the biopsy showed an aggressive change in his cancer. He comes in understandably anxious but appears engaged. He states he wants to understand his options and has expressed a clear preference to pursue one additional line of treatment, acknowledging that his disease is incurable. He does not wish to discuss hospice at this time but is open to palliative care support.

   - Olaparib 200 mg twice daily is discontinued effective today given confirmed visceral and systemic progression with histologic and molecular evidence of resistance. No BRCA2 reversion mutation makes PARP inhibitor re-challenge non-indicated.

2. NEW SYSTEMIC THERAPY — CARBOPLATIN/ETOPOSIDE:
   - Following multidisciplinary tumor board recommendation, patient will be initiated on carboplatin AUC 5 IV on Day 1 plus etoposide 100 mg/m2 IV on Days 1, 2, and 3 every 21 days. Regimen chosen to address the treatment-emergent neuroendocrine component, as in small cell/NE prostate cancer and consistent with published data for t-NEPC (Aparicio et al.; Beltran et al.).
   - Treatment planning labs (CBC, CMP, creatinine-based carboplatin dose calculation using Calvert formula) to be obtained prior to Cycle 1.
   - Cycle 1 target start date within the next 7-10 days pending labs and patient readiness.
   - Pre-chemotherapy hydration and anti-emetic protocol will be employed (ondansetron, dexamethasone pre-treatment; lorazepam as needed for anticipatory nausea).
   - Filgrastim (G-CSF) prophylaxis discussed — given his baseline anemia, low-normal counts, and multiple prior lines of therapy, we will plan G-CSF support (pegfilgrastim on day 4 of each cycle) to reduce febrile neutropenia risk.
   - Patient verbalized understanding of expected side effects including myelosuppression (especially anemia, which is already present), nausea/vomiting, alopecia, fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, and infection risk.

3. CONTINUE ADT:
   - Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months continued. Next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Continue castrate testosterone suppression for maintained hormonal control of the AR-positive component.

4. CONTINUE DENOSUMAB (XGEVA):
   - Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks continued for skeletal-related event prevention given extensive bone metastatic disease. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continue. Monitor renal function and electrolytes. Dental hygiene reminder documented — patient last saw dentist 6 months ago; avoid invasive dental procedures if possible.

5. ANEMIA MANAGEMENT:
   - Current Hgb 8.9 g/dL. With olaparib discontinuation, we anticipate some recovery in marrow function, though bone marrow infiltration cannot be excluded.
   - Repeat CBC in 2 weeks. Transfusion threshold: Hgb < 8 g/dL or symptomatic at any level. Patient instructed on symptoms requiring urgent attention.
   - Bone marrow biopsy deferred at this time — clinical picture is consistent with multifactorial anemia; proceed with carboplatin/etoposide and reassess hematologic parameters over next 4-6 weeks.
   - Hematology co-management continues. Dr. [Hematologist] notified of olaparib discontinuation and upcoming chemotherapy.

6. PAIN MANAGEMENT:
   - Current pain management: scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg every 8 hours plus as-needed oxycodone 5 mg for breakthrough pain.
   - Reassess pain control at next visit. If pain worsens, would escalate to scheduled low-dose long-acting opioid with breakthrough dosing. Palliative care will assist in pain optimization.
   - Spinal cord compression precautions discussed with patient — he has been thoroughly counseled on symptoms requiring emergency evaluation (new lower extremity weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, rapidly worsening back pain). Written instructions provided.
   - Radiation oncology referral placed for palliative radiation to the right hip/femoral neck region and/or lumbar spine if pain becomes refractory to pharmacologic management. This can be coordinated with the chemotherapy schedule.

7. PALLIATIVE CARE INTEGRATION:
   - Formal palliative care consultation requested and accepted today. Palliative care will be co-managing symptom burden including pain, fatigue, anorexia/weight loss, and anxiety going forward.
   - Social work consult also placed for caregiver support resources for the patient's daughter.
   - Advance care planning: Patient has a completed healthcare proxy designating his daughter. POLST form will be reviewed and updated with palliative care team. DNR/DNI discussion deferred per patient's wishes; he states he wants to revisit after beginning next treatment.

8. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT:
   - Albumin 3.2 g/dL, weight loss of 6 lb over 6 weeks — nutritional compromise is present and has implications for chemotherapy tolerance.
   - Dietary consult placed. Encourage high-protein, high-calorie diet. Appetite stimulant (mirtazapine 7.5 mg at bedtime) considered — initiated today, with additional benefit of anxiolytic effect.
   - Oral nutritional supplement (e.g., Ensure Plus) recommended twice daily.

9. MOLECULAR AND CLINICAL TRIAL DISCUSSION:
   - The newly detected PIK3CA H1047R mutation is potentially actionable. No PI3K/mTOR inhibitor is currently FDA-approved for mCRPC in this context, but clinical trials are available. Patient interested in clinical trial options after assessing carboplatin/etoposide tolerance. Will review available protocols at our institution.
   - NTRK, ALK, RET, FGFR and other targetable alterations not detected on the NGS panel.
   - No tumor mutational burden or microsatellite instability profile that would support checkpoint immunotherapy.

TECHNETIUM-99m MDP WHOLE BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT

TECHNIQUE: Planar whole body images were acquired in anterior and posterior projections approximately 3 hours following the intravenous administration of 25 mCi of Tc-99m MDP. Supplemental SPECT/CT images of the lumbar spine and pelvis were obtained to better characterize areas of abnormality.

FINDINGS:

Skull and Cervical Spine: No abnormal foci of radiotracer uptake. Normal calvarium and facial bone activity.

Thoracic Spine: Physiologic distribution of tracer throughout the thoracic vertebral column. No focal abnormalities identified. Normal rib uptake bilaterally.

Lumbar Spine: There are two discrete foci of markedly increased radiotracer uptake at the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies. These demonstrate intense, focal uptake in a pattern consistent with osteoblastic metastatic involvement. SPECT/CT correlation confirms the lesions are centered within the vertebral bodies with involvement extending into the posterior elements at L3. No associated cortical destruction to suggest purely lytic activity. No significant epidural extension on the limited CT component.

Pelvis: A well-defined focus of moderately increased tracer uptake is present in the right iliac wing, mid-portion, without involvement of the sacroiliac joint. Additionally, there is a distinct focus of increased uptake at the right femoral neck, immediately below the femoral head, with uptake slightly asymmetric to the contralateral side. SPECT/CT demonstrates both lesions to be intraosseous without frank cortical breakthrough, though the femoral neck lesion warrants clinical attention given anatomical weight-bearing implications.

Sacrum and Coccyx: No pathologic uptake identified. Normal symmetrical sacroiliac joint activity.

Upper Extremities: No abnormal foci. Symmetric uptake at the shoulders, elbows, and wrists consistent with mild degenerative change of no clinical concern in this context.

Lower Extremities: The right femoral neck lesion described above. Remainder of the bilateral lower extremities show no additional sites of abnormal uptake. Normal uptake at the knees and ankles bilaterally.

Soft Tissues: Physiologic renal excretion of tracer with normal bilateral renal uptake. Bladder activity present as expected. No focal soft tissue uptake of significance.

IMPRESSION — BONE SCAN:
1. Multiple foci of increased radiotracer uptake consistent with osseous metastatic disease in the clinical setting of high-grade prostate adenocarcinoma. Sites of involvement include the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck.
2. No evidence of widespread or "superscan" pattern of metastatic disease.
3. The right femoral neck lesion, while appearing intraosseous without cortical breakthrough on SPECT/CT, is noted in a weight-bearing location and warrants orthopedic/clinical review.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT acquisition of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of intravenous iohexol contrast (100 mL, Omnipaque 350). Oral contrast was administered. Axial images were obtained at 1.25 mm slice thickness with multiplanar reconstructions in coronal and sagittal planes.

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or parenchymal nodule identified. The aorta and great vessels are patent without aneurysmal change, though mild atherosclerotic calcification is present, compatible with the patient's age and cardiovascular risk profile. No pericardial effusion.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present, wall within normal limits, no cholelithiasis. No biliary ductal dilatation.

A few small retroperitoneal nodes measuring up to 7 mm (short axis) are present, well below the threshold for concern. Mesenteric fat is normal without lymphadenopathy.

Bowel: No bowel obstruction or wall thickening. Appendix is visualized and appears unremarkable.

Ascites: None.

CT PELVIS:

Prostate and Seminal Vesicles: The prostate gland is enlarged, measuring approximately 45 cc in volume. There is asymmetric bulging of the right posterolateral contour of the prostate with loss of the normal fat plane between the prostate and right seminal vesicle, consistent with extracapsular extension. Both seminal vesicles appear thickened and asymmetrically hyperdense relative to expected normal signal, with the right seminal vesicle more prominently involved. The left seminal vesicle is also abnormal, with loss of the intervening fat plane at the prostate-seminal vesicle junction. These findings are consistent with bilateral seminal vesicle invasion.

Bladder: No bladder wall thickening. No intravesical mass or filling defect. Bladder is mildly underdistended but appears normal.

Rectum: No perirectal mass or lymphadenopathy. Rectum is normal in caliber.

Lymphadenopathy: A right obturator lymph node is enlarged, measuring 1.2 cm in short-axis dimension, with a rounded, mildly heterogeneous appearance and subtle central loss of the fatty hilum, morphologically suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement in this clinical context. No contralateral obturator, external iliac, or common iliac lymphadenopathy. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Vessels: Normal caliber iliac vessels bilaterally. Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the aortic bifurcation and proximal common iliacs.

Osseous Structures: The previously described sclerotic foci at L3, L4, the right iliac wing, and the right femoral neck correspond to the areas of increased tracer uptake seen on bone scan, with the CT appearances showing mixed sclerotic and mildly expansile character consistent with osteoblastic metastatic disease. No pathologic fracture.

Soft Tissues: No pelvic soft tissue masses separate from the prostate. Mild bilateral inguinal fat prominence without discrete lymphadenopathy.

IMPRESSION — CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS:
1. Locally advanced prostate adenocarcinoma with features of extracapsular extension and bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, as described.
2. Solitary right obturator lymph node enlargement with morphologic features suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement (short axis 1.2 cm).
3. No evidence of visceral metastatic disease — specifically, the liver, lungs, and adrenal glands are clear of metastatic lesions.
4. No additional pelvic, retroperitoneal, or mediastinal adenopathy identified.
5. Osseous findings at L3, L4, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck correspond to sites of abnormal radiotracer uptake on the concurrent bone scan, consistent with osteoblastic skeletal metastases.

OVERALL IMPRESSION:
Imagings are consistent with metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma with skeletal metastases involving L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck; and regional nodal disease with a suspicious right obturator lymph node (1.2 cm). No visceral (hepatic, pulmonary, or adrenal) metastatic disease is identified. Locally advanced primary disease with extracapsular extension and bilateral seminal vesicle invasion is confirmed on CT.

SOMAT IC ALTERATIONS DETECTED — CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

1. BRCA2 — p.Ser1982Argfs*22 (c.5946delT)
   Variant Type: Frameshift deletion
   VAF: 78%
   Classification: Pathogenic (somatic; same sequence as germline)
   Interpretation: The markedly elevated somatic VAF (78%) compared to the germline heterozygous state (~50%) is consistent with somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA2 locus — i.e., the wild-type allele has been lost in the tumor through copy-neutral LOH or segmental deletion, resulting in biallelic BRCA2 inactivation. This confirms complete functional loss of BRCA2 in the tumor (homologous recombination deficiency, HRD). Biallelic BRCA2 loss is a predictive biomarker for response to PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib) in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) per FDA-approved indications (PROfound trial data); it also confers sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy.

2. TP53 — p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Variant Type: Missense (hotspot)
   VAF: 42%
   Classification: Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Gain-of-function hotspot mutation in the DNA-binding domain of TP53, one of the most frequently encountered TP53 mutations in human cancers. p.R248W abrogates sequence-specific DNA binding and is associated with dominant-negative suppression of remaining wild-type p53 alleles and potential gain-of-function oncogenic activity. TP53 mutations in prostate cancer are enriched in high-grade (Grade Group 4-5) and metastatic disease and correlate with more aggressive clinical behavior, treatment resistance, and poorer prognosis. The VAF (42%) is consistent with heterozygous somatic mutation without apparent LOH.

3. PTEN — Homozygous Deletion
   Variant Type: Copy number loss (homozygous deletion)
   VAF/Copy Number: 0 copies (deletion confirmed across multiple exons; no residual signal)
   Classification: Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Complete biallelic loss of PTEN, a critical negative regulator of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. PTEN deletion occurs in approximately 40-50% of metastatic prostate cancers and is associated with Grade Group 4-5 disease, resistance to androgen receptor-targeted therapy, and activation of the PI3K pathway as an escape mechanism. Co-occurrence with BRCA2 biallelic loss and TP53 mutation defines a high-genomic-risk phenotype. PTEN loss may have implications for sensitivity to PI3K/AKT inhibitors (investigational in prostate cancer; e.g., ipatasertib in combination with abiraterone, IMPAKT trial context).

4. RB1 — Heterozygous Deletion
   Variant Type: Copy number loss (heterozygous, single-copy deletion)
   VAF/Copy Number: 1 copy (monoallelic)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Monoallelic RB1 deletion detected. Complete biallelic RB1 loss is strongly associated with treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC) transdifferentiation, particularly when co-occurring with TP53 mutations. While currently only one allele is deleted (heterozygous), this finding — in the context of concurrent TP53 p.R248W — raises concern for evolving genomic instability and potential future neuroendocrine transdifferentiation under therapeutic selection pressure. Monitoring for neuroendocrine markers (chromogranin A, NSE) and clinical features is warranted.

5. MYC — Copy Number Gain
   Variant Type: Copy number amplification
   Copy Number: 6 (diploid reference: 2)
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: MYC amplification/gain at chromosome 8q24, observed in approximately 20-30% of metastatic prostate cancers. MYC overexpression drives cell cycle progression, ribosome biogenesis, and metabolic reprogramming. In the setting of androgen receptor signaling, MYC cooperates with AR transcriptional programs. MYC gain is associated with resistance to hormone therapies and a more proliferative tumor phenotype.

6. CDK4 — Copy Number Gain
   Variant Type: Copy number gain
   Copy Number: 4
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: CDK4 gain on chromosome 12q14. CDK4 is a key regulator of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint, phosphorylating RB1 to release E2F transcription factors. CDK4 amplification, in the setting of concurrent RB1 heterozygous deletion, may further compromise cell cycle regulation. CDK4/6 inhibitors are not currently approved in prostate cancer but represent a rational investigational target in this molecular context.

7. FOXA1 — p.R265Q (c.794G>A)
   Variant Type: Missense
   VAF: 31%
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: Recurrent activating mutation in FOXA1 (Forkhead Box A1), a pioneer transcription factor that regulates androgen receptor chromatin binding and transcriptional activity. FOXA1 mutations, including p.R265Q, are among the most frequently mutated genes in primary and metastatic prostate cancer (occurring in ~10-15% of metastatic cases). This mutation in the Wing2 domain has been shown to alter DNA binding specificity and promote AR-independent transcriptional programs, potentially contributing to endocrine resistance.

8. SPOP — p.F133V (c.397T>G)
   Variant Type: Missense (hotspot)
   VAF: 18%
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic (somatic)
   Interpretation: SPOP encodes a substrate-recognition subunit of a CUL3-based E3 ubiquitin ligase. Hotspot mutations in the MATH domain (including p.F133V at codon 133) impair interaction with substrates including SRC-3, BRD4, DEK, TRIM24, and AR co-regulators, resulting in their aberrant stabilization. SPOP mutations define a molecular subtype of prostate cancer (the SPOP-mutant subtype, ~10-15% of tumors) with distinct biology. Interestingly, SPOP mutations are mutually exclusive with ETS gene fusions (e.g., TMPRSS2-ERG) in most cases, consistent with the absence of TMPRSS2-ERG fusion in this tumor. The relatively low VAF (18%) may reflect a subclonal event.

MOLECULAR SUMMARY & INTEGRATED INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This tumor demonstrates a high-genomic-risk molecular profile consistent with Gleason Grade Group 5, metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer. Key features:

  • Biallelic BRCA2 inactivation (germline frameshift + somatic LOH): The dominant actionable finding. This confers HRD-positive status and is associated with sensitivity to PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib — FDA-approved in mCRPC setting) and platinum-based chemotherapy. In the current mHSPC setting, PARP inhibitor use is under active investigation (e.g., PROpel, TALAPRO-2, MAGNITUDE trials for abiraterone + PARP inhibitor combinations).

  • TP53 p.R248W (gain-of-function hotspot) + PTEN homozygous deletion: Dual loss of critical tumor suppressors. This combination is associated with aggressive disease biology, shortened response to ADT-based therapies, and possible PI3K pathway activation (relevant given PTEN loss).

  • RB1 heterozygous deletion + TP53 mutation: Early harbingers of potential treatment-emergent neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (t-NEPC). Complete biallelic RB1 loss combined with TP53 mutation is the canonical genomic signature of t-NEPC. Clinical and biomarker surveillance (chromogranin A, NSE, LDH) is recommended during treatment.

  • MYC gain, CDK4 gain, FOXA1 p.R265Q, SPOP p.F133V: Collectively define a proliferative, endocrine-resistant tumor phenotype with potential co-drivers of therapeutic escape.

  • TMPRSS2-ERG fusion absent; ETS-negative tumor: Combined with SPOP mutation (typical in ETS-negative subtype), this is consistent with a SPOP-mutant molecular subtype.

  • MSS, TMB-low, PD-L1 CPS 2: Immunotherapy not supported by current biomarkers.

NOTE ON GERMLINE FINDING: The identification of a germline pathogenic BRCA2 variant has important clinical implications beyond oncologic management, including risk assessment for family members. Genetic counseling referral is strongly recommended. Cascade testing of first-degree relatives (children, siblings) should be offered.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, presenting for his 12-month on-treatment follow-up visit while on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone acetate/prednisone. He presents today for routine PSA and testosterone monitoring, disease status assessment, and ongoing toxicity management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male — correction, 69-year-old male — with a diagnosis of stage IV metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma established approximately 12 months ago. At the time of initial diagnosis, his PSA was markedly elevated at 187 ng/mL. Staging workup at that time demonstrated extensive osseous metastatic disease involving the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck, as well as a single right obturator lymph node measuring 1.2 cm on CT. There was no evidence of visceral metastases at diagnosis. Biopsy demonstrated high-grade adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, with perineural invasion and androgen receptor-positive immunophenotype, without neuroendocrine differentiation. Germline testing performed at diagnosis revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with a hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome pedigree (his brother had pancreatic cancer, sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer). Somatic NGS at diagnosis further demonstrated TP53 p.R248W, PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 heterozygous deletion, and MYC gain — a genomically high-risk profile consistent with his aggressive clinical phenotype.

Approximately 12 months ago, the patient was initiated on intensified systemic therapy with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months for androgen deprivation, combined with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally once daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily, consistent with the LATITUDE and STAMPEDE trial data supporting intensified first-line therapy in high-volume or high-risk mHSPC. Castrate testosterone levels were confirmed at 6 weeks (< 20 ng/dL). The patient had an excellent initial PSA response: PSA declined from 187 ng/mL to 3.2 ng/mL at 3 months and reached a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 6 months.

Today at his 12-month assessment, the patient reports overall reasonable tolerability of his current regimen. He notes mild, persistent fatigue that he rates as 3/10 in severity and describes as a general lack of his former energy level — he was previously active as a retired steelworker and now finds that he tires more easily with household activities. He reports no worsening of back pain and no new bone pain. He has not experienced any falls. He does endorse ongoing mild hot flashes associated with castration therapy, occurring several times daily and mildly disruptive to sleep. No cognitive complaints. No urinary symptoms of concern beyond long-standing post-void dribbling from his underlying prostate pathology. No hematuria.

With respect to the abiraterone-related side effect profile, he endorses mild ankle swelling, which has been intermittently present. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations. His blood pressure was noted to be elevated at his last visit with his primary care physician and required adjustment of his antihypertensive regimen (his lisinopril dose was increased approximately 2 months ago, with good subsequent blood pressure control per his report). He has been taking his prednisone 5 mg twice daily consistently and denies symptoms of adrenal insufficiency. No GI complaints attributable to abiraterone or prednisone (no significant dyspepsia, heartburn, or nausea). His type 2 diabetes remains well-controlled per his endocrinologist; he is on metformin and his last HbA1c was 6.9%. He continues calcium 1000 mg and vitamin D 1000 IU daily for bone health in the setting of ongoing ADT-related bone loss.

He had restaging bone scan imaging performed approximately 6 months ago (at the 6-month mark) which was not yet available at our last encounter; those results are reviewed today and are discussed in the Imaging section below.

The patient is accompanied today by his wife, who confirms the history and expresses that he has been "doing reasonably well" though notes the fatigue is more prominent than before treatment. The family remains engaged and well-informed regarding the treatment goals (disease control, prolongation of time to castration resistance) and the palliative intent of therapy in the metastatic setting.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 69-year-old male with stage IV metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), harboring germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant and a genomically complex somatic profile (TP53, PTEN loss, RB1 loss, MYC gain). He is now 12 months into intensified ADT with leuprolide plus abiraterone/prednisone.

1. DISEASE STATUS — METASTATIC PROSTATE CANCER, RESPONDING TO THERAPY:
The patient has had an excellent response to date. PSA has declined from 187 ng/mL at baseline to a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at 6 months and remains low at 1.1 ng/mL today. The marginal uptick from 0.8 to 1.1 ng/mL does not fulfill PSA progression criteria; I would characterize this as a minor fluctuation in the context of ongoing excellent disease control. Testosterone confirmed castrate (< 20 ng/dL), ensuring that the hormonal backbone remains intact. Restaging imaging from the 6-month mark demonstrates partial response in both the osseous disease (sclerotic healing changes, decreased uptake) and in the nodal compartment (right obturator node decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm). No new metastatic lesions at the 6-month scan.

Current treatment: Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (next injection scheduled today/this visit), abiraterone 1000 mg orally daily on empty stomach, and prednisone 5 mg BID.

Next restaging imaging: Bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis planned in approximately 6 months (18-month on-treatment mark), or sooner if PSA rises more substantially or new symptoms develop. No PSMA PET indicated at this time given current disease control.

2. PSA MONITORING:
PSA to be rechecked in 3 months. If PSA continues to rise on 3 consecutive measurements, we will need to confirm castrate testosterone and evaluate for castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC). Given this patient's genomic profile (BRCA2 germline loss, TP53, PTEN, RB1 alterations), I have a heightened level of concern about the potential for earlier-than-average development of castration resistance; I will monitor closely.

I would note proactively: in the event of future CRPC progression following abiraterone, second-line options we would consider include enzalutamide or a PARP inhibitor given the biallelic BRCA2 loss (PROfound trial data). These options will be discussed with the patient at the appropriate time.

3. HYPERTENSION — ABIRATERONE-RELATED EXACERBATION:
Blood pressure is now well-controlled at 132/78 mmHg following the uptitration of lisinopril to 20 mg and addition of amlodipine 5 mg. Continue current antihypertensive regimen. Will recheck blood pressure at next visit. Patient counseled to continue home blood pressure monitoring and follow up with PCP as needed.

4. FATIGUE — GRADE 1, ADT-RELATED:
Ongoing Grade 1 fatigue is expected in the setting of castration therapy. The patient is not anemic at a level requiring intervention (Hgb 11.6 g/dL). ADT-related fatigue is multifactorial (loss of androgens, mild anemia, sleep disruption from hot flashes). Recommendations: maintain physical activity as tolerated (light walking, resistance exercise for sarcopenia prevention and fatigue mitigation), optimize sleep hygiene, consider cognitive behavioral approaches if fatigue worsens. No dose reduction or treatment interruption warranted at this time. Hot flashes: patient declines pharmacologic intervention for vasomotor symptoms at this time (was offered venlafaxine or gabapentin); will revisit as needed.

5. MILD LOWER-EXTREMITY EDEMA — ABIRATERONE/MINERALOCORTICOID EFFECT:
Grade 1 bilateral ankle edema, consistent with known abiraterone/mineralocorticoid excess effect. Potassium normal, blood pressure now controlled. Recommends continued dietary sodium restriction. If edema worsens or is symptomatic, may add low-dose spironolactone or recommend compression stockings. Will monitor.

6. BONE HEALTH:
Patient on ongoing ADT — at significant risk for accelerated bone mineral density loss leading to osteoporosis and fracture risk, independent of osseous metastatic disease. Continue calcium 1000 mg daily (split dosing) and vitamin D 1000 IU daily. DEXA scan has not yet been performed at our center — will order a DEXA scan to establish a baseline BMD, given that this patient is now 12 months into continuous ADT. Will refer for DEXA and discuss bisphosphonate or RANK-L inhibitor therapy (e.g., denosumab 60 mg SQ every 6 months for osteoporosis prevention, separately from any future bone-metastasis dosing regimen) depending on T-score results.

7. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
HbA1c 6.9% — well-controlled. Ongoing monitoring with endocrinology/PCP. Note: prednisone can modestly elevate glucose; continue current metformin regimen. No change needed.

8. GERMLINE BRCA2 — FAMILY COUNSELING:
I have again discussed the implications of his pathogenic germline BRCA2 variant for first-degree relatives. His adult children have been offered genetic counseling referral. Proactive testing of family members recommended. This does not alter his own immediate treatment plan today.

9. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND GOALS OF CARE:
ECOG performance status 1 today. Patient is living independently, engaged with family, and maintaining reasonable quality of life. Goals of care reaffirmed: patient understands that therapy is palliative in intent and that the goal is prolonged disease control and maintenance of quality of life. No change in goals at this visit. No hospice discussion indicated at present given stable, responding disease.

RETURN TO CLINIC: 3 months for PSA and testosterone recheck, clinical assessment, and toxicity review. Sooner as needed if symptoms develop.

Time spent: approximately 40 minutes, more than half of which was spent counseling and coordination of care.

His most recent PSA at the 12-month visit was 1.1 ng/mL, and restaging imaging at approximately 18 months showed improvement of osseous lesions with sclerotic changes and reduction of the right obturator lymph node from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm.

However, over the past 3 months, his PSA has been rising on serial measurements:
- PSA 4 weeks ago: 2.6 ng/mL
- PSA 8 weeks ago: 1.8 ng/mL
- PSA today: 4.7 ng/mL

This represents three consecutive rising PSA values with a minimum 50% rise above the nadir of 0.8 ng/mL (current value is 4.7 ng/mL, which is nearly 6-fold above nadir), fulfilling accepted criteria for biochemical PSA progression. Testosterone level drawn today confirms ongoing castrate state at less than 20 ng/dL, establishing the diagnosis of castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC).

Of note, the patient has been reporting increasing back pain over the past 4 to 6 weeks, predominantly in the lumbar region, consistent with the known sites of disease at L3 and L4. He rates the pain as 4-5/10 at worst, partially controlled with scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg three times daily. He has not required opioid analgesics. He denies any new neurologic symptoms, saddle anesthesia, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or leg weakness that would suggest cord compression.

He has been compliant with abiraterone and prednisone throughout. He denies missed doses. He has continued leuprolide injections on schedule, with most recent injection given 6 weeks ago.

He denies fever, chills, hematuria, dysuria, dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, or significant change in appetite or weight.

---

No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, hematochezia. No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary obstruction. No unintentional weight loss. No new lymphadenopathy noted by patient. No skin rash.

(documented in prior notes; summarized here for completeness)

2. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) — hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome
3. Hypertension — on amlodipine 10 mg daily and lisinopril 20 mg daily (lisinopril dose increased at 12-month visit for blood pressure optimization)

Vital Signs: BP 138/82 mmHg (right arm, seated), HR 68 bpm (regular), RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Temperature 36.7°C, Weight 83.4 kg (BMI 27.3). Weight is essentially unchanged from prior visit (+0.6 kg over 3 months).

LABORATORY RESULTS

Today's results (drawn prior to visit):
- PSA: 4.7 ng/mL (nadir was 0.8 ng/mL; prior values 1.8 → 2.6 → 4.7 ng/mL over 3 months)
- Total testosterone: <20 ng/dL (castrate range confirmed)

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent imaging: Restaging bone scan and CT abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 6 months ago (at approximately 18 months of therapy, age 69.5 years). At that time, the previously identified osseous lesions at L3, L4, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck showed improved appearance with sclerotic changes consistent with treatment response. No new bone lesions were identified at that time. The right obturator lymph node had decreased from 1.2 cm to 0.7 cm. No visceral metastases.

Given today's documented PSA progression with three consecutive rising values (and confirmed castrate testosterone), restaging imaging is warranted and is being ordered today — specifically a PSMA PET/CT given the sensitivity advantages over conventional bone scan/CT in this setting, particularly for low-volume disease detection.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 70-year-old male with high-risk metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5, T3b N1 M1b) with germline BRCA2 biallelic loss and complex somatic genomics (TP53 mutation, PTEN loss, RB1 loss on original somatic panel), presenting with biochemical PSA progression on ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone after an initial robust response lasting approximately 18-20 months. Testosterone is confirmed castrate. This fulfills criteria for castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC).

1. CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER — NEW DIAGNOSIS
   The defining features are unequivocal: three consecutive PSA rises above nadir (0.8 → 1.8 → 2.6 → 4.7 ng/mL over approximately 3 months) while testosterone is in the castrate range (<20 ng/dL). This is a biochemically progressive CRPC, currently without confirmed radiographic progression on recent imaging (though imaging is now 6 months old and must be repeated). Clinically, the patient has worsening lumbar pain at the sites of known disease, which may also reflect radiographic progression.

   Plan:
   a. Continue abiraterone/prednisone for now pending restaging imaging, as there is no consensus on exact timing of switch — we will not discontinue until imaging confirms progression and we have a clear next line.
   b. Order PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) for complete restaging — superior to conventional bone scan/CT for identifying extent of disease in CRPC, especially given BRCA2-mutated biology which can associate with more aggressive disease spread. Will facilitate treatment planning.
   c. Following imaging review, plan to transition to enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily as next line of therapy — appropriate given CRPC with predominantly bone/nodal disease (no current visceral crisis), prior abiraterone use (cross-resistance with re-challenge is expected), ECOG PS 1. Will hold abiraterone when enzalutamide is started.
   d. Of critical note: this patient carries a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant with confirmed somatic LOH (biallelic loss). Per PROfound trial data and FDA approval, olaparib is highly appropriate in the post-second hormonal agent setting. We will reserve this as the next-next line after enzalutamide progression, given the expectation that sequential ARSI therapy (abi → enza) may have limited benefit (abiraterone-to-enzalutamide cross-resistance is real, but enzalutamide still represents standard sequencing in the absence of visceral crisis). BRCA2 status makes olaparib a very strong subsequent option.
   e. Bone health: With new CRPC designation and likely ongoing disease activity, initiate denosumab 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks for skeletal-related event prevention (together with calcium 1200 mg/day and vitamin D 2000 IU/day already on board). Will check calcium before first dose; creatinine acceptable. Educate patient on jaw osteonecrosis risk — dental clearance to be obtained prior to first dose if not done recently (patient to call dentist).
   f. Orthopedic/radiation oncology input: Given known right femoral neck lesion, consider prophylactic radiation therapy discussion if weight-bearing pain worsens. Recommend gentle activity as tolerated. Continue cane use.

2. BACK PAIN (GRADE 2) — LIKELY DISEASE-RELATED
   Lumbar pain at L3-L4 correlating with known osseous metastases. Pain is manageable with scheduled acetaminophen at present. Will reassess after imaging.
   - Continue scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID.
   - If pain escalates, will consider low-dose opioid analgesic (oxycodone IR 5 mg q4-6h PRN as a starting point) and/or referral to palliative care pain management service.
   - PSMA PET/CT will be informative regarding stability vs. progression at these sites.
   - Neurology/spine surgery to be consulted if any signs of cord compression emerge — patient and wife given return precautions in writing (saddle anesthesia, lower extremity weakness, bowel/bladder changes = ER immediately).

3. BONE HEALTH AND SKELETAL METASTASES
   As noted, initiating denosumab. Alkaline phosphatase mildly elevated (142 U/L), consistent with bony disease activity. No frank hypercalcemia (not checked today formally, but metabolic panel is within normal limits). Dental clearance required before first denosumab dose — patient reminded today, to schedule dental appointment within 2 weeks.

4. ANEMIA (HGB 12.1 g/dL, MILD)
   Hemoglobin mildly decreased from prior value. Likely multifactorial: ADT-associated anemia, chronic disease effect. Not clinically significant at this time. Will monitor with next visit CBC. No intervention needed at present.

5. DIABETES MANAGEMENT — HBA1C 7.3%
   Slight worsening, partially attributable to chronic low-dose prednisone. Will alert PCP and endocrinology. Continue metformin. Dietary counseling reinforced.

6. HYPERTENSION
   BP today 138/82 — borderline. Currently on amlodipine 10 mg + lisinopril 20 mg. Will ensure internist/cardiologist monitoring continues. No changes today.

7. GOALS OF CARE AND PATIENT/FAMILY EDUCATION
   Extensive discussion today with Mr. Kowalski and his wife regarding the meaning of castration resistance. Explained that although this is a significant milestone in the disease course, there remain multiple meaningful lines of therapy available, including enzalutamide and, importantly, olaparib given his BRCA2 mutation. Explained the goals of each subsequent therapy are disease control and quality of life preservation, not cure. Patient and wife understood and expressed willingness to continue active treatment. Patient feels his quality of life remains reasonable and he wants to pursue further therapy. These discussions were conducted in a calm, unhurried manner with an interpreter service offered (declined — wife assists with translation of any complex medical terminology).

   Patient provided written materials regarding CRPC and bone-protective therapy.

8. FOLLOW-UP
   - PSMA PET/CT to be scheduled within 1-2 weeks; patient will be called with appointment.
   - Follow-up office visit in approximately 3-4 weeks after imaging available to review results and transition therapy (abiraterone → enzalutamide).
   - CBC, CMP, PSA, testosterone at next visit.
   - Dental clearance for denosumab — patient to arrange within 2 weeks and report back.
   - Continue leuprolide IM every 3 months (next injection at 6-week mark from last dose, i.e., approximately 4 weeks from today — nursing to schedule).
   - PCP contact: will send note regarding HbA1c 7.3% and CRPC diagnosis update.
   - Genetic counseling referral for family members to be renewed — patient's adult children were offered germline BRCA2 testing; reminder given today.

Time spent counseling patient and family: approximately 40 minutes. More than 50% of visit time spent in counseling and coordination. Complexity: high.

EXAMINATION: Gallium-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT, whole body with diagnostic CT

CLINICAL INDICATION: 70-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Initially diagnosed with stage IV (T3b N1 M1b) prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), with bone and nodal metastases. Treated with ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone for approximately 24 months with initial biochemical and radiographic response. Now with PSA rising from nadir of 0.8 ng/mL to 4.7 ng/mL over three consecutive measurements in the setting of castrate testosterone levels, fulfilling criteria for castration-resistant progression. Known germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant. Study performed for restaging and disease characterization prior to next-line therapy.

TECHNIQUE: Intravenous administration of Ga-68 PSMA-11 (approximately 185 MBq). Whole-body PET imaging was obtained approximately 60 minutes post-injection from the vertex of the skull to mid-thighs, with supplemental imaging through the pelvis and proximal femora. Co-registered low-dose CT was performed for attenuation correction and anatomical localization. A separately acquired diagnostic CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast was obtained in the same session. PET image quality is good. No adverse reaction to the radiopharmaceutical or contrast agent.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No PSMA-avid intracranial or calvarial lesions. No PSMA-avid cervical lymphadenopathy. The parotid and submandibular glands demonstrate expected physiologic PSMA uptake bilaterally. Thyroid gland unremarkable.

CHEST:
Lungs: No focal pulmonary nodules or masses on low-dose CT. No pleural effusions. The mediastinal and hilar structures are unremarkable. No PSMA-avid pulmonary or pleural lesion.

Mediastinum and Lymph Nodes: No PSMA-avid mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes by CT size criteria.

Sternum and Ribs: There is a new focus of moderately intense PSMA avidity involving the right posterior 7th rib at the costovertebral junction. CT shows subtle sclerotic density at this site without a discrete lytic component. A second new focus of PSMA avidity is identified at the left posterior 9th rib, again with subtle underlying sclerotic change on CT. These foci were not identified on the prior conventional bone scan approximately 12 months earlier and are consistent with new osseous metastatic deposits.

THORACIC SPINE:
At the T10 vertebral body, there is a new focus of moderately intense PSMA uptake (SUV max approximately 6.4). Corresponding CT demonstrates patchy sclerotic density involving the mid-vertebral body without significant cortical disruption or epidural extension. This was not present on prior imaging and represents a new osseous metastasis. No other PSMA-avid thoracic vertebral lesions are identified. No pathological fracture. No epidural or paraspinal soft tissue component.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: No PSMA-avid hepatic lesions. The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic abnormality on contrast-enhanced CT.

Spleen, Pancreas, Adrenal Glands, Kidneys: Unremarkable. The adrenal glands show no PSMA-avid lesions. No adrenal enlargement. Physiologic renal excretion of PSMA tracer is noted bilaterally. No renal masses.

Retroperitoneal and Mesenteric Lymph Nodes: No PSMA-avid retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy above the aortic bifurcation. No pathological adenopathy by size criteria in this distribution.

LUMBAR SPINE:
The previously identified lesions at L3 and L4 vertebral bodies demonstrate interval increase in PSMA avidity compared to the prior restaging bone scan and CT. The L3 lesion shows heterogeneous, moderately intense tracer uptake (SUV max approximately 8.1), and the L4 lesion demonstrates similarly increased uptake (SUV max approximately 7.3). On CT, both lesions appear predominantly sclerotic but are more prominent in extent compared to prior imaging. There is no associated epidural soft tissue component and no spinal canal compromise. These findings are in keeping with progressive disease at known sites of osseous involvement. No new lumbar vertebral lesions below L4.

PELVIS:
Lymph Nodes: The right obturator lymph node, previously measuring 1.2 cm at diagnosis and 0.7 cm on restaging during treatment response, has now enlarged to 1.4 cm in short axis on CT. It demonstrates increased PSMA avidity (SUV max approximately 9.2) compared to prior imaging, confirming active nodal metastatic involvement and interval progression at this site. No other pelvic lymph nodes meet size criteria for pathological enlargement, though a small right internal iliac lymph node measuring 0.8 cm demonstrates faint PSMA uptake that is indeterminate.

Bladder: Normal. Expected physiologic PSMA tracer accumulation in the urinary bladder. Post-treatment changes in the pelvis are noted, consistent with prior prostate disease history. No residual PSMA-avid signal in the prostate bed.

Right Sacral Ala: A new, discrete focus of moderately intense PSMA uptake is identified in the right sacral ala (SUV max approximately 5.8). CT demonstrates faint sclerotic density at this site. This focus was not identified on prior bone scan and is consistent with a new osseous metastatic deposit. No cortical breakthrough. No associated soft tissue mass.

Right Iliac Wing: The previously identified lesion at the right iliac wing demonstrates continued PSMA avidity, appearing stable to minimally increased in intensity compared to prior bone scan. CT shows predominantly sclerotic changes. No new lesion in the contralateral iliac wing.

Right Femoral Neck: The previously identified lesion at the right femoral neck demonstrates persistent PSMA avidity. CT appearance is stable with sclerotic morphology. No pathological fracture or cortical breach.

LOWER EXTREMITIES:
No additional PSMA-avid foci in the visualized bilateral femora or proximal tibiae.

PSMA-NEGATIVE LESIONS:
No lesions suspicious for malignancy on CT that lack corresponding PSMA avidity are identified. No PSMA-negative visceral masses or lymph nodes of concern.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Osseous disease progression compared to prior restaging bone scan. New PSMA-avid lesions are identified at the following sites not previously documented: T10 vertebral body, right posterior 7th rib, left posterior 9th rib, and right sacral ala — representing at least four new bone metastases. Previously identified lesions at L3 and L4 vertebral bodies show increased PSMA avidity consistent with active progression at these sites.

2. Stable osseous metastatic foci at the right iliac wing and right femoral neck, with sclerotic morphology suggesting prior treatment effect, though these sites remain PSMA-avid.

3. Interval enlargement and increased PSMA avidity of the right obturator lymph node (now 1.4 cm, previously 0.7 cm on prior restaging CT), consistent with nodal disease progression. A small right internal iliac lymph node with faint PSMA uptake is indeterminate and warrants follow-up.

4. No visceral metastases. No hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other soft tissue metastatic deposits identified.

5. No PSMA-negative lesions to suggest dedifferentiated or neuroendocrine subclone not captured by PSMA-based imaging at this time.

Overall, the findings are consistent with progressive osseous and nodal metastatic disease in the setting of established castration-resistant prostate cancer, with multiple new bone sites and nodal enlargement. The extent of PSMA-avid disease suggests systemic progression and these findings were communicated to the treating oncology team at the time of interpretation.
